## SERMONS OF HENRI BVLLINger, vpon the Apocalipſe reuealed to the holy Apoſtle and Euangeliſt S. Iohn.

## ☞ Of the authour of the boke of Apocalipſe, of the argument and partes therof: Finally of the ſondry vſe, and moſte profitable commoditie of the ſame. ¶ The firſt Sermon.

THe Prophetes of God of the olde Teſtament,[note 1] were God his meſſengers to the people, expoſitours of Moyſes or of Gods lawe, and euen eccleſiaſtical preachers, which applied the doctrine taken out of the lawe of God, to the people and tymes wherin they liued to thedifiyng of Congregations. And they al with one [illegible]ccorde haue chiefly handled two thinges in their Sermos. For firſt the[illegible] haue reproued the corrupt maners of al ſtates [illegible]n their tyme, alledging the rule and preſcript of Gods law: And exhorting all men to repentaunce vnto God moſte acceptable. And to many that were vncurable they threatened all kynde of plages, which they ſetting forth with all beau [illegible]ie of ſpeache, ſhewed them plainly to be ſene with the eye, if [illegible]aply they myght ſo be made afrayd, and healed. Secondly [illegible]hey promyſed and ſet forth by the mouth of God, the Lord Chriſt, the true Meſſias: Whom alſo they deſcribed liuely, [illegible]nd with all his holy churche, teaching the faith in Chriſt, and [illegible]oth what good thinges are prepared for the faithfull in Chriſt: And alſo what be the true duties of pietie and godlines. Neither haue they concealed ſuche thinges as were nedefull [illegible]o be knowen of Antichriſt: Admoniſhing vs moſt dilligetly [illegible]hat we ſhould beware of that wolfe, or rather the moſt depe [illegible]ongeon of all abominations, and that we ſhould ſtand faſt [illegible]n the ſincere faith of Chriſt. &c.

I haue taken vpo me, through the help of God and your [illegible]rayers, to expoud vnto you the Apocalipſe,[note 2] which is a doc [illegible]rine concerning the matters of Chriſtes churche reuealed [illegible]rom heauen of Chriſt in glory, and a ſom of all godly religion, an expoſition and brief declaration of the Prophetes, and a prophecie of the newe Teſtament and ſtory of the church

Howbeit, foraſmuche as this booke is diſpiſed of ma[illegible] good and well learned men, and for that all men for [illegible] moſt part ar fully perſwaded that it is an vnprofitable bo[illegible] I wyll ſpeake here ſomwhat of the ſame matter.

[note 3]Firſt many abhorre this boke for this cauſe only that is full of viſions, types and figures, for they ſuppoſe ho[illegible] this becometh not the Euangelicall and Apoſtolicall d[illegible] trine of the newe Teſtament. But by the ſame meane a go[illegible] part of Daniell ſhould be caſt away: Whiche neuerthele[illegible] is commended to vs of Chriſt hymſelfe in Mathewe.[note 4] [illegible] greateſt matter of all, namely the callyng of the Gentils, ſhewed to S. Peter by a viſion, as appereth in the actes.

[note 5]And the Prophet Ioel ſayd alſo, howe the people of [illegible] newe Teſtament ſhould ſee viſions. And ſo doeth the bl[illegible] ſed Apoſtle S. Peter, expounde the ſame place in the A[illegible] of the Apoſtles, ſpeakyng of the people of the newe Teſ[illegible] ment. And our Sauiour Chriſt in the Goſpell, propoun[illegible] and declared to the people the moſte part of the miſteries parables, and in maner by fayned fables, as they call th[illegible] And how much thinke you doe theſe viſions, types, and [illegible] gures of S. Iohn, differ from the ſame? This kynd of ſp[illegible] doth not darken matters, but maketh them plaine: And [illegible] keth much for the efficacitie and perſpicuitie, and for the c[illegible] firming of the memory. For by this meane, matters be [illegible] only declared with wordes, and heard with the eares: [illegible] are ſet forth alſo to be ſene of the eies, and after a ſort be fi[illegible] in the memory.[note 6] Many for this cauſe attribute much to pa[illegible] ting: But I ſuppoſe that I may much more rightli attribu[illegible] very much to this maner of ſpeaking and teaching, wher[illegible] the matter is vttered, not by a coulored, domme, and dead pa[illegible] ting, but as it were with a ſpeaking and liuely maner ſet fo[illegible] to be ſene of the eies. Whiche is therfore propounded, th[illegible] men myght rightly and exactly vnderſtand the ſame. Alb[illegible] therfore that this whole boke in a maner conſiſteth of viſio[illegible] and figures:[note 7] Yet ſhall we in dede, through the inſpiration [illegible] Gods grace, ſhew in our expoſition, that all that ſame m[illegible] keth for the perſpicuitie and plainenes, and not for the obſe[illegible] ring or darkening of moſt high and godly matters. I wy[illegible] bring my expoſition out of the very ſcriptures, by coferring ioyning therunto the rule of faith and charitie. I will ſearche out the circumſtaunces, the thinges that follow and go before: I wil bring ſimilitudes and diſſimilitudes: I wil adde alſo ther vnto the experience of things, and the faith of hiſtories. Which maner of expounding the ſcriptures all interpretours haue always graunted to be ſound and true.[note 8] If better thinges ſhalbe reuealed to others, I wil gladly after the precept of the apoſtle geue place vnto my betters. For I offer theſe my doings to be wayed of the godly, vpon this conditio, that they ſhuld trie al things, and that which they ſhal find to be good, to hold faſt.

Secondly they obiect, that aſwel new men as olde,[note 9] of no ſmal authoritie, haue both doubted of this boke, and of the authour therof, and alſo haue contemned it, as full of fables, and vnworthy to be rekened canonical. Let thoſe that ſo thinke geue me theſame libertie, I deſire them, which they vſurpe tho the ſelues, and thinke it lawful. For if the boke of the Apocalipſe [illegible]hould therfore ſeme worthy to be contemned, for that ſome notable men, both old and new, haue doubted of the authoritie [illegible]herof: Why may it not recouer his authoritie againe, if I [illegible]hew that the beſt doctours of the church both old and new, haue [illegible]ad a right good opinion of this boke? And here to thintent [illegible] wold diſſemble nothing at al, I am not ignorant that doctor Martin Luther, a man right notably learned,[note 10] in his firſt edi [illegible]ion of the new Teſtament in dutch, with a ſharp preface ſet [illegible]efore, hath ſticked this boke as it wer with a dagger. Howe [illegible]e it good and wel learned me were offended with him for this [illegible]is iudgement, which in the ſame found lack both of wit and mo [illegible]eſtie. The ſame ma therfor waying al things more vpright [illegible]y and diligely, what time he corrected his dutch Bible. &c. My [illegible]orſhipful maiſter ſemeth alſo not to haue ſet very much by his boke, and to haue aſcribed it not to Iohn the Apoſtle, but to Iohn who thei called a diuine. But herein there is no doubt but that he folowed plainly Eraſmus of Roterda, who is his annotations vpon the new Teſtamet. In al the Greke copies, [illegible]aith he, that I haue ſene, the title was not of Iohn thapoſtle, [illegible]ut of Iohn the diuine. Eraſmus addeth that amongeſt the Grekes and certen old wryters, men doubted of this authour, whiche thing he declareth by the teſtimonies of Euſebius, and Hierom, of whoſe opinion ſhalbe ſpoken ſtreightways. But the exempler or Spaniſhe coppie whiche is ſet foorth after the faith of the moſte auncient and approued Grekes, exhibiteth to vs ſuche a title of ths boke. [illegible]: That is the Apocalipſe of the holy Apoſtle and Euangeliſt S. Iohn Diuine. For the auncient writers ſay, how S. Iohn the Apoſtle and Euangeliſte, for his excellent wryting of the ſonne of God, was commonly called, Diuine. Wherof it followeth, tha[illegible] this title doeth attribute and not take from S. Iohn this booke. Certes Aretas was alſo, a Greke Aucthour, an[illegible] byſhop of Ceſaria: Of the auncientes, ſaieth he, certen hau[illegible] plucked this Apocalipſe from the tongue of that welbeloue[illegible] Iohn, aſcribing it to another: but it is not ſo. For that ſam[illegible] Gregory, which as well as he, is called a Diuine, accom[illegible] teth this amongſt thoſe ſcriptures, whiche vtterly want a ſuſpicion of counterfeiting: ſaying, as the Apocalipſe of S[illegible] Iohn teacheth me. And the ſame man a litle after: But th[illegible] this boke was written by the mouth of the holy ghoſt, S. B[illegible] ſil, Cyril, Papias and Hippolytus,[note 11] fathers of the church, a mete men to be credited. Thus ſaith he. What ſhal we ſay[illegible] that Eraſmus confeſſeth, that the conſent of the worlde, an authoritie of the churche to be of ſuche force with hym, th[illegible] he dare not refuſe this boke?

Let vs heare now the iudgement of that moſte excellen[illegible] beſt man D. Ioh. Oecolampadius,[note 12] the moſte faithfull p[illegible] ſtour of the churche of Baſill, and excellently learned in [illegible] prophetical and in al the Canonicall ſcriptures, concerning this boke, which he left vs written in the xii. Chap. of thei[illegible] [illegible] cond boke of his commentaries vpon Daniel: But S. Io[illegible] the Paraphraſte or expoſitour of the Prophetes, ſaith he, ([illegible] how muche he doth attribute to this our authour) whom maruel, why certen with ſo raſſhe a iudgement doe reiect as a dreamer, and franticke, and an vnprofitable wryter[illegible] the church: Where neuertheleſſe he propoundeth and ſette[illegible] forth very many of the moſt ſecret and hid things of the old[illegible] [illegible] ſtament and of the Prophetes. But thoſe great men do b[illegible] wray, what a wening they haue of themſelues: Whoſe iu[illegible] gementes I would verely rather contemne as prophan[illegible] than I would caſt away ſuch a treaſure. I could here bringe forth goodly teſtimonies of other newe wryters, but that I make haſte to the iudgementes of the auncient fathers.

The eldeſt of all after the Apoſtles,[note 13] whoſe wrytynges as yet remayne, Iuſtine and Ireney, the noble Martirs of Chriſt, aſcribe this boke to Iohn the Apoſtle. For Euſebius in the xviii. Chapter, of the fourth boke of the Eccleſiaſticall ſtory. Iuſtine, ſayth he, mentioneth of the Apocalypſe of Iohn, ſaying playnly that it is the Apoſtles. S. Hierom alſo in the lyfe of bleſſed Iuſtine wryteth, that Iuſtine expounded the Apocalypſe of S. Iohn, but the ſame expoſition remayneth not ſo farre forth as I knowe. The ſame authour wryteth that Ireney[note 14] ſet forth the Apocalypſe of S. Iohn, with a commentary, whiche alſo is not to be had. He hym ſelfe, who is red to haue lyued about the yeare of our Lorde, 160. Witneſſeth playnly in the fift boke againſt the Valentinians, that this reuelation was exhibited to Iohn the Apoſtle a lytle before his dayes. We alledge certen wordes of his in the xiii. Chapter of this boke. Tertullian,[note 15] who lyued about the yeare of our Lorde two hundred and xx. in the fourth boke againſt Marcion. Albeit ſayth he, that Marcion refuſeth the Apocalypſe of Iohn, yet ſhall the order of Byſhoppes rekened vp to the very begynnyng, affirme S. Iohn to be authour therof. In graue matters, and reaſoning againſt heretickes, he vſeth gladly the teſtimonies of this boke.

The ſame thinges are alſo recited of the bleſſed Martyr S. Cyprian,[note 16] vnder the title of Iohn the Apoſtle in this Epiſtles, treatiſes and Sermons. Euſebius alſo in the xviii. Chapter of the fift booke of the Eccleſiaſticall hiſtorie,[note 17] ſheweth, that Appollonius a moſte auncient wryter vſeth the teſtimonies of the Apocalypſe of S. Iohn:[note 18] And lykewyſe Theophilus Byſhop of Antioche. Whiche he affirmeth in the xxiiii. Chapter of the fourth boke of the Eccleſiaſticall ſtory. Alſo Origen,[note 19] a great man in the churche of God in the xxv. Chapter of the ſixt boke of the ſame Euſebius. And he wrote, ſayeth he, the Apocalypſe, whiche reſted vpon the Lordes breſte. &c.

I haue hytherto recited the opinions of the moſt auncie[illegible] Martyrs and Doctours of the chriſten churche, touchyn[illegible] the Apocalypſe, I meane Iuſtine, Ireney, Tertullian, [illegible] prian, Appolonius, Theophilus, and Origen: I wyll ſhor[illegible] ly after brynge yet moo iudgementes boeth of the [illegible] and Latin wryters, of moſte authoritie in the churche, ag[illegible] yng with the myndes of them that we haue alledged [illegible] therto: Howbeit I wyll fyrſt touche briefly ſuche thyng as Dioniſius of Alexandria,[note 20] left wrytten of the ſame boo[illegible] in the fyue and twenty Chapter of the ſeuenth booke of [illegible] ſebius, whome I ſuppoſe they haue followed, as many[illegible] after hym haue ſpoken againſt this booke. He ſayeth h[illegible] diuerſe that were his predeceſſours, dyd vtterly repr[illegible] and reiecte this booke. Neyther hydeth he the cauſe, w[illegible] they ſo dyd, for that the kyngdome of Chriſte is affyrm[illegible] therin to be earthly. Wherunto doubtleſſe they reſer[illegible] that precious citie, and the reſte whyche vnder terreſtr[illegible] kyndes, figured ſpirituall thynges. Whiche when we [illegible] in the treatynge therof haue diſſolued, declaryng thys bo[illegible] not to edifie the earthely kyngdome of Chriſte, but a ſpi[illegible] tuall and celeſtiall, no man I trowe, wyll reiecte a good a[illegible] Godly booke, for bycauſe certen abuſyng the teſtimon[illegible] therof, geue vnto it a wrong ſenſe.

Heretickes haue wraſted verey many places of [illegible] ſcripture,[note 21] to the defence of their errour, ſhoulde therfore authoritie of the ſcripture it ſelfe be brought in doubte? I[illegible] fauoureth nothing at all the Chiliaſtes or Millenaries this boke: He geueth them no weapons.

Euſebius ſaieth very well in the ende of the third bo[illegible] ſpeakyng of Papias, the firſt authour of the Millenaries He thought, ſaieth he, that after the reſurrection Chri[illegible] ſhould reigne here corporally with his a thouſand yea[illegible] in earth. Whiche I ſuppoſe he thought for that he vnde[illegible] ſtode not well the Apoſtles woordes, neither that he co[illegible] dered not well thoſe thynges that were ſpoken of hym [illegible] der figures, for that he was indewed with a ſmall iudg[illegible] ment.

But in the meane tyme Dioniſius hym ſelf, I, ſayth h[illegible] dare not reiecte this booke. He addeth by and by, that he thynketh it not yet to be the booke of Iohn the Apoſtle, but of ſome other, but yet who that ſhould be, he knewe not. He gathereth alſo by certen coniectures, by the phraſe of ſpeach, and handlyng of the booke, and by the vnlykenes of wytte, that this boke ſhould be an other mans, than his that wrote the Goſpell, and Epiſtle. But ſeing that the argumentes of the ſtory and Epiſtle be ſo diuerſe, that neyther they two be lyke, and the argument of the booke of Reuelation moſte diuerſe of all: Why ſhoulde it ſeme maruell, though it agreeth not with them in all thynges?

This can no man denie, but that in conſent of doctrine there is great agremet. The Epiſtle to the Hebrewes ſemed to many to fauour in the ſyxt and tenth Chap. The Nouatians or Catharites. The diuerſitie of ſtyle was noted to differ from the reſt of Saint Paules Epiſtles. But if we ſhould ſo iudge of holy ſcriptures, I knowe not what ſhuld be firme and ſure enough. Leauing therfore this diſputation in ſuſpence, I wyll nowe procede to bryng foorth the iudgementes of other olde wryters concerning this boke.

Euſebius[note 22] ſurnamed Pamphilus Byſhop of Ceſarea, lyuing in the tyme of great Conſtantine the Emperour, and a moſte dilligent reader of olde wryters, whome many ſuppoſe in deminiſhing the authoritie of this boke to fauour them, ryght eloquently in the eighteth Chapter of the third booke of his ſtory, tauntyng the Tyranny of Domitian, affirmeth that Iohn exiled into Pathmos, wrote there hys Reuelation.

And where other Hiſtoriographers doe alſo the ſame. He againe in the xxiiii. Chapter in the thyrd booke, concerning the Apocalipſe, ſaith he, the opinion of men is diuerſe, ſome approuing, and others reprouing the ſame. Again whe he ſhould bring forth his opinion touching the Canon of the new Teſtament in the xxv. Chapter. He ioyneth the Apocalipſe with the bokes vndoubted: although he diſſembleth not that he wyll ſhew in another place what other men thinke therof. Whileſt he this performeth, he recopteth many more and better, which iudged the Apocalipſe to be of S. Iohn thapoſtle, and imbraced it as a moſte Godly booke, than thoſe which denied or reproued the ſame.

[note 23]Epiphanius Byſhop of Salamine in Cypres, a Greke Authour alſo doeth manifeſtly aſcribe this boke to S. Iohn the Apoſtle. Reade, that he hath left wrytten againſt Thelogians in the xv. hereſie. And S. Hierome attributeth very muche to this Epiphanius.[note 24] And S. Hierome hym ſelf[illegible] aſcribeth this boke to Iohn the Apoſtle, to Paulinus: Th[illegible] Apocalypſe of S. Iohn, ſayth he, hath ſo many Sacramentes, as it hath wordes. Moreouer Philaſtrius Byſhop o[illegible] Griria,[note 25] whome S. Auſten ſayth he ſawe with S. Ambro[illegible] at Millan, accompteth them for Heretickes, that reiecte th[illegible] Apocalypſe of Iohn, and ſaye that it is not of Iohn the Apoſtle, but of Cerinthus an Hereticke. Verely S. Ambroſ[illegible] [note 26] hym ſelfe alledgeth in his bokes teſtimonies of the Apocalypſe, vnder the name of S. Iohn the Apoſtle.

S. Auſten[note 27] imbraced this boke as Apoſtolicall: and re[illegible] the ſame to his churche, leauing certen treatiſes vpon th[illegible] ſame. Primaſius alſo Byſhop of vtica in Africke, expou[illegible] ded the ſame as Apoſtolicall.[note 28] Of Bede and the reſidue o[illegible] that ſorte, I ſpeake nothyng, ſynce his opinion is know[illegible] to all men.[note 29] Andreas alſo Byſhop of Ceſaria, wrote vpo[illegible] this boke: As Aretas reporteth in his commentaries, who opinion I declared before.

Me thinketh I haue ſufficiently confirmed the authoriti[illegible] of this boke, againſt them that diminiſhe the ſame. But th[illegible] ſame ſemeth ſtrongeſt of all, that the thinge it ſelfe, and t[illegible] handlyng therof proueth, that it did procede from the Ap[illegible] ſtle. Whiche thyng we ſhall proue in the treatiſe it ſelfe. But in caſe thoſe bleſſed fathers in their tyme did expound the Apocalypſe to theyr churches,[note 30] why ſhould it not be lawfull for vs alſo in our time, to expounde it to our men, whic[illegible] are in the ende of the worlde, where nowe all thynges [illegible] more fully then they were than accomplyſhed? Yea the[illegible] thynges ſerue moſte chiefly for vs and for our tyme, fyne that we trauell and be exerciſed vnder Antichriſte.

In vayne therfore many prattell, that this boke is obſcu[illegible] and can not bee vnderſtande: And for the ſame cauſe to b[illegible] read in the Churche without any profit or fruite.

For to ſpeake nothyng hereof, that nothyng is ſet forth in holy ſcripture, whiche hath not an excellent fructe: Neyther muſt we by and by diſpayre of the true vnderſtanding, although at the fyrſt ſight of the holy ſcripture it be obſcure, whiche is opened of God hymſelfe, and to be opened, is obteyned by prayers, and Godly exerciſes: Certes we are not ignoraunt that many had rather nothyng were ſpoken of Antichriſte, to the intent he myght reygne more careleſly, and they them ſelues be leſſe ſubiecte to perilles. But Chriſt commaundeth vs to trouble hym. Let vs therfore go forward in the worke of the Lorde.

And where it offendeth them,[note 31] that Iohn maketh little mencion or none of Chriſt, where notwithſtanding the maner of Apoſtles is, to intimate alwayes Chriſt, and the grace of redemption: We ſuppoſe this ſame boke more throughly loked vpon, to proue the contrary. Whoſe argument nowe I wyll recite.

The Prophet Zacharie in the thirde Chapter,[note 32] obiecteth the whole miſterie of Chriſte to all mens eyes in a moſte euident figure to be ſene. For he ſeeth Ieſus the hygh prieſt, appareled in vile garmentes, and lyke a coale that is taken out of the fyre, to ſuffer muche contradiction of the Deuyll: by and by the ſame to haue put of the vyle clothynge, and put on whyte garmentes, to be glorified, and proclamed kyng and prieſt and Sauiour of all.

This fygure the Apoſtle and Euangeliſt S. Iohn expoundeth: And firſt in deede the Goſpell beyng ſet forth, he deſcribeth Chriſt in vyle apparell, howe muche contradiction beyng taken he ſuffered of the wycked, tyll at the length he was nailed to the croſſe. He toucheth there alſo his glory: Whiche notwithſtanding, the Apocalypſe therto annexed, he declareth more at large, ſhewyng the ſame vnto vs in a whyte garment and in glory, howe verely after this humblyng being exalting, he obteyned a name aboue all names: And now being in glory, howe he worketh neuertheleſſe in the churche, the ſauiour of all the faithfull in the churche. In his Epiſtle he commendeth this whole miſtery of pietie and beateth in to all men.

For the whole booke is diuided into ſyxe partes.

[note 33]1 For firſt is ſet the title with the begynnyng and ſome o[illegible] the worke, and with a briefe narration: And all this in th[illegible] firſt part of the firſt chapter.

2 Secondly from the myddes of the firſt Chapter to th[illegible] fourth Chapter, is deſcribed Chriſt reignyng in glory, o[illegible] the ryght hand of the Father, and is declared, howe he i[illegible] conuerſaunt in the Churche by his ſpirite, and miniſtery o[illegible] hys woorde. What thynge he teacheth from heauen, and what is the ſyncere doctrine of the churche: what is alſo th[illegible] reparing of Churches that are fallen, and the preſeruatio[illegible] of theſame.

3 Than from the fourth Chapter to the xii. Chriſt ſtill admoniſheth his Churche diligently by ſeuen Seales, and ſeuen trompettes, what thynges ſhall happen to the Church[illegible] all the whiche are moſte iuſtly gouerned of God hym ſe[illegible] by the Lambe Chriſt.

4 Moreouer from the xii. Chapter to the xv, is more fully deſcribed the conflict of the Churche with the olde Serpent, and with the olde and newe beaſte. Where alſo the f[illegible] myſhe Tyranny both old and newe, and verey Antichriſt hym ſelfe is trymly paynted foorth in his coulours: N[illegible] withſtandyng that afterwarde alſo theſe thynges are agay[illegible] more playnly declared.

5 And from the fiftenth Chapter to the two and twenti[illegible] Chapter, are recited the paynes and tourmentes of Antichriſte, and Antichriſtians, and the deſtruction of the ſam[illegible] and the condempnation of all the wycked. Alſo the Iudg[illegible] Chriſte is ſette foorth, and the proceſſe of an external iudgement is fygured. There is alſo rehearſed the triumphe, Ioie, and rewarde of Sainctes. Where alſo heauen it ſelfe is opened to bee ſeene of our eyes, that now[illegible] we maye by faythe looke in to the ſame. The depthe o[illegible] Hell is opened, that we maye looke in to it alſo: And maye take good heade that we bee not throwen thether headlong.

6 Finally in the ende of the xxii. Chapter, followeth the concluſion, and commendation of the woorke, with the ſealing vp of the ſame.

And here I wyll not hyde an other diuiſion of thys woorke not to bee contempned,[note 34] which I ſee the expoſitours haue followed. For fyrſt they rehearſe the Title and begynnyng. After they annexe the whole woorke it ſelfe, diuided by ſeuen viſions. And in dede the ſeuenth numbre is moſte frequent, and as it were peculiar to this booke. Finally they adde to the concluſion of the woorke in a maner compriſed in the laſt Chapter. And theſe viſions be compaſſed within their limites.

In the thre fyrſt Chapters the firſt viſion is expounded exhibityng Chriſt vnto vs reigning in glory, gouerning, ordering, correcting, and preſeruing his churche.

The ſecond viſion beginneth in the fourthand, reacheth to the eight Chapter. That ſetteth forth God him ſelfe and his Chriſte to be loked vpon, whoſe moſte iuſte gouernment of all thynges in the worlde it commendeth, and openeth ſeuen Seales.

The thyrde viſion hath ſeuen Aungelles ſoundynge with ſeuen Trompettes. Whiche treatiſe ſtretcheth to the xii. Chapter.

The fourth Viſion ſheweth the fighte of the woman wyth the Serpent, and ſetteth foorth to vs the olde ſeuen headed, and the newe two horned beaſte to be ſeene, the deſcription of Antichriſte, and this in the xii. xiii. and xiiii. Chapter.

In the fift viſion ſeuen Aungels power out ſeuen vials of Gods wrath, vnto the xvii. chapter.

From thence begynneth the ſixt viſion, and extendeth to the xxi. Chapter, and diſputeth of the moſte iuſt iudgement of God againſt Babylon, the whore of Babylon, and the Antichriſtians, finally againſt all wycked and impenitent perſones.

The ſeuenth and laſt viſion propoundeth to the eyes of all the faythfull, the glory and blyſſe euerlaſtyng[illegible] of Sainctes. And verely thys diuiſion of the woorke hath a great grace and affinite with the reſt of the thynges, which in this boke are all in a maner treated by the ſeuenth nubr[illegible] Let the reader followe whiche he wyll.

[note 35]Nowe of theſe thynges euery man may perceyue the thys booke is altogether Apoſtolicall, and exceadynge profitable to vs all, eſpecially whome the ende of the world[illegible] hath ouertaken. And this booke ſhall bee eaſier for vs, f[illegible] that all thynges nowe are in a maner accomplyſhed.

Daniell was thought to haue tolde of ſtarke dreame[illegible] when before the Monarchies, he prophecied the Mona[illegible] chies. But after thoſe thynges were accompliſhed, whic[illegible] he prophecied, he ſemed vnto many to haue compiled an h[illegible] ſtory. The ſelfe ſame, I am ſure, thou wylt iudge alſo: thys ſame boke of Saint Iohn. A fewe profittes only of m[illegible] ny we ſhall recite.

Firſt we haue in this booke a moſte full diſcription Chriſt, reignyng in glory, our kyng I ſaye, and Byſhop And howe he gouerneth the Churche, and is the Sauio[illegible] of all faythfull. We haue alſo a moſte gallaunte deſcri[illegible] tion of Chriſtes Churche, and howe the ſame maye be bu[illegible] ded, repared, and maynteyned. Than haue we a perf[illegible] deſcription of Antichriſt, of his members, and Synagog[illegible] of his counſelles, craftie deuiſes, kyngdome, crueltie, a[illegible] deſtructions of the ſame: From the whiche it byddeth [illegible] beware. Moreouer we haue an abrydgement of Hiſtor[illegible] from Chriſtes tyme, vnto the worldes ende.

Finally an abſolute and certayne prophecie of thyng[illegible] to come, that we neede not to haue the prophecies of M[illegible] thodius, Cyrill, Merline, Briget, Nolhard and certen triflers

Furthermore we haue a great conſolation and comfort of the churche in aduerſitie, whyleſt boeth we ſee th[illegible] Lambe to open the Seales, and that all thynges are do[illegible] by Gods prouidence, and that there is an ende of euylle[illegible] And that the churche ſhall bee euermore, in diſpitee of all th[illegible] Deuyls in hell. Laſt we haue a moſte plentifull and ſure do[illegible] trine of the Iudge and laſt iudgement, of paynes and of t[illegible] wardes.

All theſe thinges I ſay, ſhal the treatiſe it ſelfe ſhewe plainly, for our edefiyng through Ieſus Chriſt our Lord.

## OF THE TITLE OF THE whole worke, and expoſition therof. The ſecond Sermon. ☞ I ſaid the whole boke was conteined in ſixe partes.Thre membres of the firſt part. Now muſt we loke on the firſt part: Which hath chiefly three members: The title, beginning, and brief narration. For this preſent we wyll only ſpeake of the Title, whiche is thus.

THe reuelatio of Ieſu Chriſt, [note 36] whiche God gaue vnto him, for to ſhew vnto his ſeruauntes, thinges whiche muſt ſhortly come to paſſe: And he ſent and ſhewed by his aungell vnto his ſeruaunt Iohn, which bare recorde of the word af God, and of the teſtimony of Ieſus Chriſt, and of all thinges that he ſawe. Happy is he that readeth, and they that heare the wordes of the prophecie, and kepe thoſe thinges which are written therin. For the time is at hand.

This title is plentifull,[note 37] and vttereth all profitable circumſtaunces, that are to be declared in the beginnings of bokes.

Firſt is ſet the Title, or inſcription of the whole worke, that is, the Apocalipſe, or reuelation of Ieſus Chriſt, whiche verely was opened or reuealed by Chriſt Ieſus him ſelfe.

This title ſtreightway proueth,[note 38] that this worke is no mans inuention, but a godly doctrine: As that whiche was opened by our Lord, kyng, and prieſt Ieſus Chriſt, out of heauen, from the right hand of the father, executing there the office of the high Byſhop, and as yet teaching vs profitable thinges. and albeit it be called alſo the reuelatio of Iohn, yet is it chalenged to hym for none other cauſe, than for that as ſcribe he wrote and ſet it forth.

[note 39]Againe it is yet more playnly declared, from whence this Reuelation is: Euen of God hym ſelfe. For he ſaieth, which God, namely the Father, gaue vnto hym, to wytte, to Chriſt. For in the holy and bleſſed Trinitie, there is a diſtinction of perſones. And albeit that all thinges of the father, be the ſonnes alſo: And all thynges of the ſonne, the fathers lykewyſe: Yet the ſcripture mentioneth the father to geue vnto the Sonne, and the Sonne to receyue of the Father.

Whiche thynge all the auncient wryters, haue full Godly expounded, to be done by the miſtery of diſpenſation. For the Sonne receyued ſomewhat of the Father, as man whiche otherwyſe as the very Sonne of God ſayeth Father,[note 40] glorifie thou me with the glory which I had with thee, before this worlde was.

Moreouer the Sonne is the wyſdome, word, and mouth of the Father, by whome God in tymes paſte and nowe, ſpake and ſpeaketh to the Fathers, Prophetes, Apoſtles, and to the vniuerſall churche. The Father by diſpenſation gaue to his Sonne this office, that he ſhould be Byſhop. For no man hath ſene God at any tyme: The only begotten whiche is in the boſome of the Father, he hath reuealed vnto vs. Let vs knowe therfore, this ſame to be a Reuelation Diuine, whiche God the Father, louynge mankynde, hath reuealed by the only Byſhop Chriſt vnto hys Churche. And ſo it ioyneth together the Father and the Sonne, that neuertheleſſe the holy diſtinction of perſones remayneth ſafe.

[note 41]Nowe alſo is added, to what ende God the Father hath reuealed, or geuen the gyft of reuealing, to wytte, the office of prieſthod, to his Sonne, our Lorde Ieſus Chriſt: to the intent verely that the ſame beyng reuealed, he myght ſhewe it, and as it were ſet it before the eyes of his ſeruauntes, to wytte, his worſhippers, and Chriſtians, which are called the ſeruauntes of God for theyr wyllyng obedience. And as the ſeruaunt of a Lorde is a ſeruaunt, and oweth to his Lord all that he hath, or is worth: So we owe vnto God our ſelues whole, and all ours, or els we be free, and not bounde.

Here is alſo declared, vnto whom this reuelation is opened:[note 42] To all the ſeruauntes of God. If therfore thou be glad to be called the ſeruaunt of God, heare this boke, and remember it: And knowe that this boke is prepared for thee of God.

After he compryſeth in fewe wordes,[note 43] what Chriſte reuealed to Iohn, thynges that muſt ſhortly come to paſſe. The deſtenies therfore of the Churche are recited, what good and euyll thynges ſhall happen to the Godly, and lykewyſe what punyſſhementes muſt be inflicted to the wicked.

And let no man gather of this woorde, muſt, neceſſitie, as though God wrought not freely:[note 44] Or that the wycked dyd euyll, not through their owne faulte, but by Gods compulſion. Good thynges muſt be done, bycauſe God wyllyngly byndyng hym ſelfe to vs by hys promeſſe, can not but doe that he doeth and promyſeth: Neuertheleſſe he worketh frely.

Good thynges muſt be done in the Godly, for bycauſe the nature of grace and faith is ſuche, lyke as the propertie of vngodlines is to contemne and tranſgreſſe. Wherfore they muſt alſo be punyſſhed. And bycauſe the worlde is ſuche, as it is, there muſt needes bee Hereſies and calamities innumerable. And he ſayeth theſe thynges muſt ſhortly be done that are reuealed, for bycauſe certen thynges began in the very tyme of S. Iohn. And although many thynges are founde to be doone a thouſande yeares after,[note 45] yet ſayeth the Apoſtle ſaynt Peter. A thouſande yeares before the Lorde, be as it were yeſterday. Therfore this Reuelation apperteyneth to the tymes of the primatiue and laſt Churche: And declareth what thynges ſo euer ſhal happen vnto it tyll the laſt iudgement. Yea and howe it ſhall reigne for euer.

Moreouer the maner of reuealyng is alſo touched.[note 46] For Chriſt reuealed thoſe thynges, ſendyng by hys Aungell, or his Aungell ſent forth, vnto whome he gaue in commaundement what he ſhould ſaye and doe. Whereupon thys Aungell is after alſo called Chriſte, bycauſe he repreſented the perſone of Chriſte. Therfore muſt not the Aungell in this booke, but Chriſt alwayes be conſidered, the trewe Authour of all theſe thynges. And in dede the Diuinitie of Chriſt is here commended vnto vs, what tyme we hea[illegible] that Chriſt is the Lord of aungels. Wherof S. Paul to the Hebrues hath reaſoned more at large.[note 47] Moyſes in the xii.[illegible] Numbres ſetteth forth chiefly thre maners of propheciyn[illegible] or reuelation.[note 48] Firſt by viſion, of the which ſorte many are [illegible] ſcribed to Daniell, one notable to S. Peter in the x. of th[illegible] Actes, and likewyſe to S. Paul. And into this forme Inferre alſo the Apocalipſe. Secondly by dreame: of the whic[illegible] ſorte were thoſe of Pharao and Nabuchodonozer kynges wherof Ioſeph and Daniel were interpretours.[note 49] The Prophet Ioel in the ſecond chapter, mentioneth of viſions and dreames. For in the newe Teſtament alſo there be very many holy and prophetical dreames. Laſt of all Moyſes rehe[illegible] ſeth a ſkylfull expoſition, as many were made to Moyſes to the Apoſtles. Into whoſe fellowſhip the Apocalipſe commeth after a ſorte alſo, where viſions are openly expounde[illegible] Here appereth the vnſpekeable goodnes of God, whiche [illegible] many wayes procureth and worketh our ſaluation, and ſ[illegible] pleaſauntly prepared offereth it vnto vs to inioye. Vnhappy is he, that knoweth not theſe thinges.

[note 50]Beſides this, much mencion is made vnto whome Chriſ[illegible] hath opened this diuine and moſt excellent reuelation, eu[illegible] to Iohn. He commendeth hym, that is himſelf (for ſo was i[illegible] expedient for the confuting of his aduerſaries, ſeing that Pau[illegible] alſo many tymes mainteined his authoritie againſt the fal[illegible] Apoſtles) by foure Epithetes. For firſt he calleth himſelf th[illegible] ſeruaunt of Chriſt. This is the eldeſt and nobleſt title, whic[illegible] the fathers, Prophetes, and Apoſtles haue vſed. For they b[illegible] addicte and conſecrate to God. Secondly Iohn teſtified th[illegible] worde of God amongſt the Apoſtles, moſt expreſſely declaring the diuinitie of Chriſt, eſpecially where he teſtified and ſaid In the beginning was the worde.[note 51] &c. Moreouer he teſtifie[illegible] the witnes of Ieſu Chriſt: Vnder the which name the Lord[illegible] himſelf in the Goſpell, and S. Iohn in the xii. chapt. of his Goſpel, copriſed the whole Euangelicall doctrine. And was a ſeing witneſſe of all theſe thinges. For in the firſt Chapter we haue ſene, ſayth he, his glory. And in the xix. Chapter he ſawe water and bloud guſſe out of the Lordes ſyde. In his Epiſtle,[note 52] that we haue ſene, ſayth he, and haue heard. &c.

Aretas noteth, that in certen Greke copies is added, that whiche is had alſo in the Greke copie of Spayne. And what thinges ſoeuer he hath heard, and what ſo euer be, and what ſo euer muſt be done after this.

That ſame Iohn therfore is authour of this boke,[note 53] whiche as he ſawe the Lorde in fleſh vpon earth, ſo he ſawe the ſame in ſpirite reuealing theſe thinges in heauen: and propoundeth to the churche ſightes moſt certen and ſure. This Iohn was that beloued diſciple of the Lord, whiche in the laſt ſupper, reſted vpon his breſte, vnto whom in his laſt will he bequeathed his mother on the croſſe, one virgin to another.

He alone ſtode by at the aultar of the croſſe whe Chriſt died: witnes of the true death, and of our purification. He lyued til the tyme of the Emperour Traiane, which thing Euſebius in his cronicles citeth out of Ireney, in the noting of the yeare from the birth of Chriſt an hondreth and thre. Dorotheus a moſt aucient wryter, affirmeth the Iohn liued vi. ſcore yeares.

Laſt is touched alſo the profit of this godly worke or reuelation,[note 54] that hereby the readers and hearers might be prouoked to diligence. Where this boke is called alſo a prophecie. For this boke by reaſon of the telling before of thinges to come, is the prophecie, of the newe Teſtament: Moreouer a prophecie, that is an expoſition,[note 55] whiche openeth and and expoundeth the olde Prophetes. And promiſeth bleſſednes to the readers, hearers, and kepers of the things that are wrytten in this boke. Bleſſednes comprehendeth the benefites of the life preſent, ſo farre forth as the Lorde permitteth them to the godly: but chiefly of the lyfe to come. If the profit of this boke hath bene alſo ſpoken before in the firſt Sermon. And marke, that it is not enough to reade or heare this boke. It muſt be perfourmed in dede, and kept dilligently.

For the Lord ſayd alſo in the Goſpell: Bleſſed are they that heare the word of God and kepe it. They therfore that ſhall frame their lyfe after this boke, are happie. For both they flie the ſeducing of Antichriſte, abide in the faith of Chriſt, and liue for euer more. &c.

And he finiſheth the Title with an acclamation, by the whiche he moueth the hearers exceadingly: For the tyme is at hande, as thouge he ſhould ſaie: Let no man thinke here, that ſtraunge thinges, and the which concerne him noth[illegible] are tolde here, which ſhal come to paſſe at length after ma[illegible] worldes, they belong to euery one of vs. For they be wr[illegible] ten of matters that chiefly concerne vs, and euen of our o[illegible] affaires. So ſheweth he that this booke is profitable for a worldes, men, and ages. God the father by his ſonne teach[illegible] profitable thinges, and admoniſhing tyme enough and dewe ſeaſon, be prayſed worlde without end. Amen.

## OF THE BEGINNING OF THIS boke, and the Apoſtles ſalutation: wherin are declared the miſteries chiefly of Chriſt, ſecondly of our whole faith and redemption. The third Sermon.

IOhn to the ſeuen cogregatios Aſia. Grace be with you and pea[illegible] from him whiche is, and whic[illegible] was, and whiche is to come, and [illegible] the ſeuen ſpirites which are pr[illegible] ſent before his throne. And fro Ieſus Chri[illegible] which is a faithful witnes, and firſt begot[illegible] of the dead: And Lord ouer the kinges of t[illegible] earth. Vnto hym that loued vs, and waſſh[illegible] vs from ſinnes in his own bloud: And ma[illegible] vs kynges and prieſtes, vnto God his fathe[illegible] be glory and dominion for euermore. Ame[illegible] Behold he cometh with cloudes: And al ey[illegible] ſhal ſe him: And thei alſo which pearſed hi[illegible] and al kinredes of the earth ſhal wayle ouer him Euen ſo. Ame. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and thending, ſaith the Lord almight[illegible] which is, and which was, and which is to come.

[note 56]Another pece of the firſt part of this boke, conteineth t[illegible] beginning or preface, wherin is the Apoſtles ſalutation, [illegible] the whiche he diſcribeth firſt the whole miſtery of Chriſt, [illegible] condly of our faith and redemption. For ſo were the Apoſtles wont in the beginning of their writinges to compriſe a brief ſome of ſalutation: Which thing in Paules Epiſtles is euery where to be ſene. By the ſame deſcription he getteth the beneuolence and attentiuenes of al men.

The Apoſtles ſalutation or greting is nothing els,[note 57] but a bleſſing. Bleſſing is an old accuſtomed order, by the whiche the Patriarkes wiſhed of God to their children al maner of good thinges, both of body and ſoul. Which verely in Geneſis is deſcribed at large. And alſo the high prieſt had comaundement geuen to bleſſe the people: As we reade in the ſixt of Nu bres, eſpecially he commaundeth to put his name vpon the people. Therfore it is a ſuperſticion to ſay God verely, from whome euery good gifte deſcendeth fro aboue, bleſſeth, that is geueth good thinges: but miniſters or men wiſhe only. And the Lord in dede in the lawe promiſeth, that he will graut thoſe thinges to the people, whiche the high prieſtes ſhoulde wiſhe them. Therfore nother wordes nor ſhauen crownes: but the truth and power of God geue the giftes. We ought not therfore to doubt, but that God wil graut to vs alſo thapoſtolical bleſſing, that being reconciled and accepted of God we might haue peace. And firſt S. Iho repeteth his name, left we ſhuld any thing doubt of thautour,[note 58] who we ſee Chriſt to haue vſed as ſcribe and interpretour vnto al cogregatios. But he repeteth not himſelf to be that ſeruaut of god, and witneſſe or Apoſtle of Ieſu Chriſt. It ſufficed to haue heard that at the firſt beginnig. Therfore he teacheth the modeſtie and humilitie alſo, which haue obteined great giftes. Afterward he ſignifieth, to who he wryteth, and to who this boke apperteineth, to the ſeuen churches of Aſia, the names wherof he will vtter ſhortly after. And Aretas biſhop of Ceſaria, by the vii. churches, ſaith he, and by the vii. nu bre, he ſignified the multitude of churches that be in al places. So alſo Primaſius biſh. of Vtica in Affrick, expoudeth the vii. nu ber. Therfor this ſalutatio, this boke, and the whole doctrine of Ieſu Chriſt, writte by ſ. Iho,[note 59] apperteineth to the whole vniuerſal church of Chriſt throughout al the world, and in all times and ages. Wherupo it belogeth to all vs alſo as many as be of vs in the church of Chriſt. For albeit thepiſtles be intitled to the Romains and Galath. yet followeth it not therfore, that they be not ours. And he wryteth expreſſely to the churches of Aſia, not to t[illegible] churches of Hieruſalem or Iewery: that he might ſo ſhew[illegible] that the kingdome of Chriſt, is comen alſo already to the g[illegible] tiles. And as God from the beginning choſe Iſraell, in whi[illegible] he myght ſet forth a perfit example of the church, and com[illegible] wealth: ſo fro the beginning of the new Teſtamet, he choſe thoſ[illegible] ſeuen churches of Aſia, which he might ſet forth to the wh[illegible] Chriſten world. But in caſe Rome had ben ſet in the firſt pla[illegible] amongeſt the churches, as Epheſus is, good God, ho[illegible] much wold the Romiſh ſort make of it, for the eſtabiſhing[illegible] their ſupremacie.

[note 60]And the maner of the Apoſtles ſaluting wyſheth gran[illegible] peace. Grace is the fauour of the deitie, and the reconc[illegible] ment, wherby God the father for Chriſt his ſake is made[illegible] one with vs, our ſinnes pardoned, and we adopted for his chi[illegible] dren. Therof arriſeth the peace and tranquillitie of mynd[illegible] and the deſire of concorde with all men.

And here he ſheweth aboundantly, who geueth the churc[illegible] his bleſſing, that is to witte, grace, reconcilement, and pea[illegible] God, and God thre in perſons, the father, the ſonne, and th[illegible] holy ghoſt, one God in eſſence. But here he diſcerneth th[illegible] perſones very well. From him that is, to witte, the fathe[illegible] And from the ſeue ſpirites that is, from the holy ghoſt: And fro[illegible] Ieſu Chriſt, this is the diuerſitie of perſons. And the ſignification of the vnitie is, when after the proprieties of perſons d[illegible] clared,[note 61] he addeth: I am Alpha and omega. &c. And that the ho[illegible] ghoſt is ſet here in the middes, it diſordereth not the miſte[illegible] of the Trinitie: but appeareth to be an argument that he [illegible] the ſpirite as well of the father, as of the ſonne, and that h[illegible] procedeth from both. As it is alſo proued by the wordes [illegible] our lord, the xiiii.xv. and xvi. of Iohn. Here is alſo deſcribe[illegible] the whole holſome miſtery firſt of Chriſt, than of the catholike faith, and of our redemptio, ſo that herein you may find[illegible] the chiefeſt articles of the Apoſtles crede, and haue here a moſ[illegible] goodly deſcriptio of Chriſt our Lord. Hereof al me ſhall iudg[illegible] how truly ſome men ſay,[note 62] that this boke, contrary to the [illegible] ſtome of thapoſtles, maketh litle mentio of Chriſt and of faith

The father as fountain and original, of whom the ſon is ingendred, is firſt deſcribed: for that it is he whiche is, which was, and which is to come. Thoſe wordes toke Iohn out of Moyſes in the iii. and xxxiiii. chap. of Exod. and out of many teſtimonies of Eſay. And he ſaith nothing, but that God the father is an eternall enſence, which coſiſteth by and of it ſelf, and is and geueth life to all, and in all preſerueth the ſame. And that this eſſence is ſuche, that it hath bene always with out beginning. For this is it, that he ioyneth to being, or exiſting, was. He addeth, and he that ſhall come ([illegible]) which ſhalbe, and ſhal remaine euen to the ende, and to euerlaſtingnes without end. The Grekes deriue [illegible] of running, for that conning and running, he medleth with all matters: is euery where preſent, bringing help to the godly, or reſtreining and puniſhing the wycked.

And the holy ghoſt where he is but one,[note 63] for the ſeuenfolde that is all maner of grace and giftes manifold, is here called, as I may ſay, Septenarie or of the ſeuenth numbre: And from the ſeuen ſpirites ſayth Iohn, that is from that ſpirite, whiche is indewed with the ſeuenfolde grace. Thoſe diuerſe giftes are after a ſorte declared of Eſay in the xi. Chapt. and els where in the ſcriptures. He is ſayd to be in the ſight of the throne that is before the throne of God, ioyned verely in gouernement with the father and the ſonne. For the throne is many tymes vſurped for the kingdom. The holy ghoſt therfore is of the ſame glory, power, and maieſtie with God.

Now is he commen to Chriſt,[note 64] whom by his properties he deſcribeth moſte aboundantly. You know, that Ieſus is the proper name of Chriſt: which Matthew expoundeth, a Sauiour, Chriſt is the ſurname of his office and dignitie: as you would ſay, annoynted, that is, byſhop and kyng.

1 Firſt he calleth Chriſt our Lord, a faithfull wytnes,[note 65] and that out of the xlix. and l. chapter of Eſay. For he was ſent of the father to the world out of heauen, an Apoſtle, whiche ſhould teſtifie the wil of God, what he wold haue done with men. To witte that he would ſaue the worlde by his ſonne,[note 66] and by faith in him, which is obedient to the law of God. For he muſt do the will of his father. This Chriſt is a faithfull witnes, that is ſure, conſtaunt and trewe: Of whoſe doctrine no man ought to doubt. No man hath ſeene God at any tyme: The only begotten whiche is in the boſome of the father,[note 67] he hath reuealed. This therfore is the byſhop and catholick d[illegible] tour of the church. Who ſo euer diſſent from him, are to [illegible] eſchewed. Heare him, ſaith the father.

[note 68]2 He is the firſt begotten of the dead. For he died for o[illegible] ſinnes verely: and roſe again from the dead, and was made t[illegible] firſt begotten of the dead, Lorde and conquerour of death: I[illegible] whom we ſe that we ſhall alſo ryſe again, and in what ſorte. [illegible] whom the firſt of the Corinth. xv. And like as in the firſt pr[illegible] pertie he ſhaddoweth the humanitie of Chriſte, wherin h[illegible] taught alſo his deitie, in that he was the faithfull, true, and [illegible] tholique byſhop, and is yet at this day: So in the ſecond, th[illegible] articles of our belefe concerning the death of Chriſt, and h[illegible] reſurrection are confirmed. To theſe alſo may be added t[illegible] article of the reſurrection of the dead.

[note 69]3 Chriſt is prince ouer the kinges of the earth, a monar[illegible] verely, and Lord of all rulers: Whiche hath taken a name [illegible] boue all names, the Lord of aungels, and of all creatures, [illegible] whom al things be ſubiect: As thapoſtle expoudeth. Coloſ[illegible] Philip. 2. And he doeth not aboliſhe lawes and Magiſtrat[illegible] which wil be king of kinges, and Lord of lordes. For if tha[illegible] wer no kings, how ſhuld Chriſt be king of kings? The mo[illegible] ſacred Emperours, Conſtance, Conſtantine, Theodoſe, an[illegible] Iuſtinian, knew them ſelues to be clientes of Chriſt: Tha[illegible] kingdom was Chriſtes, and they to be ſubiectes. Theſe Chri[illegible] acknowledgeth for his, by who he gouerneth thoſe he ha[illegible] redemed with his bloud. They that proudly rule ouer the people, boaſt the ſelues to be lordes of althings, and acknowled[illegible] not Chriſt to be monarch ouer all, be ſtarke mad. And her[illegible] are comprehended ſuch thinges as we confeſſe in tharticles of our faith, that Chriſt aſcended into heauen, and ſitteth on th[illegible] right hand of the father: that is, that he hath receiued high p[illegible] wer of al things in heauen and earth. Ephe. 1. Act. 2.

[note 70]4 Chriſt hath loued vs with incoparable loue. For he hi[illegible] ſelf ſaith: greater loue hath no man, than that a man ſhoul[illegible] leaue his life for his fredes. This loue the Apoſtle amplefiet[illegible] in the fift to the Romains. And it was exceading great loue [illegible] moued Chriſt to come downe from heauen and be incarna[illegible] and to redeme vs by his death. With a free loue he loue[illegible] vs, prouoked by no deſert of ours. For as this ſame Iohn in his Epiſtle canonicall ſpeaketh the ſame of the father. In this is charitie, not that we haue loued God, but that he hath loued vs, and ſent his ſon a propiciation for our ſinnes: So is it to be vnderſtad of the ſon, the he hath and doth bear vs great good will, not moued thereto through our loue, wherewith we haue imbraced him. And of the fre loue to mankinde, he gaue him ſelf vnto death, and waſhed vs fro our ſinnes. For ſtreight waies is added, by his bloud.[note 71] Where thre thinges ſeme of vs to be obſerued. Firſt that Chriſt waſheth, purgeth, purifieth or cleſeth the faithful: and that moſt fully, not partly. He alluded to the waſhings of the law, which he expouded alſo. For Dauid ſaith: Pourge me with Hyſope, and I ſhalbe made cleane, waſhe me, and I ſhalbe whiter than ſnow. The ſame phraſe of ſpeache repeteth Eſay in the firſt chap. Micheas alſo ſayth:[note 72] The Lord wil returne and wil haue mercy on vs, he wil treade vnder fete our iniquities: And thou ſhalt throw into the depth of the Sea al their ſinnes. And the Lord ſaith,[note 73] I wil caſt vpon you cleane waters, and you ſhall be mundified from al your filthines. The Lord Chriſt theſe thinges accompliſhing, waſheth vs, purgeth and clenſeth throughly, aſwell from the falte as the paine. He clenſeth vs from our ſinnes,[note 74] not from one, but from al. The which thing is proued both by former teſtimonies, and again in the firſt and ſecond Epiſtle of S. Iohn. Laſt, the maner alſo of purifiyng is ſet forth, by bloud. For without the ſheding of bloud no remiſſion was made. Therfore through the mediation of death and bloudſheding there was full remiſſio of all ſinnes obteined for the faithfull.[note 75] They that bring forth any other maner of forgeuenes of ſynnes, ar iniurious to the death and bloud of the ſonne of God. And here we may ſe plainly ſet forth an article of the Apoſtolicall crede. I beleue the forgeuenes of ſynnes.

In the fift place is ſhewed theffect of our redeption and purifiyng. For Chriſt hath brought to paſſe,[note 76] that as many of vs as beleue in the father by the ſon of God, ſhuld be kinges and priſts to God and to his father. Aretas and the copie of complute reade not kinges but [illegible], that is kyngdome: the which is not red amiſſe. For we be the kingdo of God, for bicauſe God by his ſpirit, not the fleſh nor the worlde, ought to reigne in vs: And when we permitte the gouernment to the ſpirit, we be the kingdo of God: The which thing S. Paul handleth at large in the ſixt chap. to the Romains. Moreouer we be made kings that is free,[note 77] by Chriſt, that we ſhould not ſerue the deuill, th[illegible] fleſh, and the world, according to that ſaying of Zacharie, [illegible] being deliuered from the handes of our enemies, we migh[illegible] ſerue him without feare in holines and rightuouſnes befo[illegible] hym al the daies of our life.[note 78] And Chriſte hath conſecrated v[illegible] prieſtes with his ſpirite and bloud, that we ſhould offer vp t[illegible] God ſpirituall ſacrifices, our ſelues pure, prayers and pra[illegible] ſes,[note 79] and almoſdedes. For that theſe be ſpirituall oblation[illegible] Peter and Paul do teſtifie. And theſe thinges toke S. Ioh[illegible] out of Exodus: For we of the Gentils that haue beleue[illegible] haue ſucceded in the place of the people of Iſraell reiecti[illegible] Chriſt through incredulitie. And theſe thinges geue a lyg[illegible] to that article of the Crede, I beleue the holy catholick church the communion of ſainctes. For we be as many of vs as b[illegible] leue, the fellowſhip of Gods people, ſanctified through Chriſ[illegible] to the ſeruice of God. Of whome be theſe thinges hitherto.

In the ſixt place, in the diſcriptio of Chriſt he ſheweth the glory and rule is dewe vnto God alone through Chriſt [illegible] the churche for euermore:[note 80] We geue glory vnto God, wh[illegible] we aſcribe to his goodnes our ſaluation, and all goodne[illegible] not to our own ſtrength and merites. We geue hym ru[illegible] when we acknowledge hym to be Lord and head in the churc[illegible] workyng by hym ſelfe, not by the ſainctes in heauen, to wh[illegible] he hath graunted power: Not by the Pope, whom he ha[illegible] conſtitute Vicar in earth. The whole glory and rule is Chriſts

Seuenthly in the deſcription followeth the commyng [illegible] Chriſt vnto iudgemet,[note 81] and the maner of his comming. F[illegible] as a cloude toke him vp from the eyes of the Apoſtles, e[illegible] ſo ſhall he come in cloudes to iudge the quicke and the dea[illegible] The ſcripture witneſſing. And he addeth, that the eyes of a[illegible] men ſhal ſe the iudge,[note 82] euen of thoſe which haue peaſed hym Wherof we gather two thinges, firſt, that the iudgemet ſha[illegible] be vniuerſall. Wherin men arriſing ſhall ſe Chriſt with th[illegible] owne eies. An other thing, that Chriſt ſhall come to iudg[illegible] ment in the ſame fleſhe,[note 83] in the whiche he was wounded and ſticked, honge vpon the Croſſe, was buried and roſe again This place is taken out of Zacharie, and is cited alſo in S. Iohns Goſpel.[note 84] And it behoueth that his body be ſhewed to the whole world full of printes and markes, that herof may be iudged the Godly and alſo the vngodly: They that then haue beleued in ſuch a redemer: Theſe, that then haue reiected and contemned ſuche a one. Of theſe we vnderſtande that is added: And they ſhall wayle, for that in dede thei haue neglected their owne ſaluation: Which the wiſe man diſcourſeth at large.[note 85] Moreouer leſt any may ſhould doubt of thoſe thinges that are ſpoken of the iudgement, and of the lamentation of the wycked (as S. Peter ſaid,[note 86] the contemners and mockers of the iudgement ſhould be) he addeth a kynde of a confirmation, euen ſo. Amen.

In them alſo is expouded the article of the crede of Chriſt that ſhall iudge the quicke and the dead. He concludeth this place with theſe wordes: I am Alpha and Omega, that whiche followeth (the beginning and end) is omitted in ſome copies: As though that interpretation of that ſame, I am Alpha and Omega, crept in out of the margent. It is a prouerbe of S. Iohn the Apoſtle, I am Alpha and Omega. Heretickes, as Baſilides and Valentine, were wonderfully delighted in letters. But againſt thoſe lettered Heretickes Iohn ſpeaketh plainly by the mouth of Chriſt. I am Alpha and Omega. If any thing ought to be aſcribed to letters, I am al this whole that euerlaſting vertue, eſſence, and eternitie. For the ſenſe is, that God is the beginning and ende, that is eternall, vnſpeakeable, beſt and greateſt. Thoſe things are repeted: He that is, which was. &c. Which were expouded before: There is added, almightie. For hereby is declared the vnitie and maieſtie of God, of whom the Trinitie was opened alſo before. Hereby alſo the authoritie of this boke is confirmed, the authour wherof is ſhewed to be that God eternal and almighty. To whom be glory.

## ¶ Of the Narration of this boke, where alſo is diſcourſed of the place and tyme, and of the authour of this Reuelation. The fourth Sermon.

I Iohn your brother and companio in tribulation, and in the kingdo and patiece which is in Ieſu Chriſt, was in the Ile of Pathmos for the word of God, and teſtimony of Ieſ[illegible] Chriſte. I was in the ſpirite on the Sonday and heard behind me a great voice, as it ha[illegible] bene of a trompe, ſaying: I am Alpha and Omega, the firſt and the laſt. That thou ſee[illegible] wryte in a boke, and ſend it to the congregations whiche are in Aſia, vnto Epheſus, an[illegible] vnto Smyrna, and vnto Pergamos, and Thyatire, and vnto Sardis, and vnto Philade[illegible] phia, and vnto Laodicia.

[note 87]The laſt place of the firſt ſheweth vnto vs a brief narrati[illegible] wherin the Apoſtle S. Iohn declareth the tyme and place this Reuelation, and by whoſe commaundement he wrou[illegible] ſent the ſame to the ſeuen churches in Aſia.

And againe now the third time is the name of Iohn rehea[illegible] ſed. He ſawe vndoubtedly, that there would be ſome which to the ende they might take away the vſe and fruite of th[illegible] boke, wold doubt of the authour: Againſt whom he repet[illegible] and reiterateth his name ſo oft, leeſt we ſhould doubt and lac[illegible] the great commoditie of ſo worthy a boke.

[note 88]He addeth to his name certen thinges, whiche inſtruct[illegible] touching the ſtate of the Apoſtle, and certen profitable ma[illegible] ters. Firſt he calleth him ſelf a brother, namely of thoſe ſ[illegible] churches, and of al ours: As where I haue admoniſhed yo[illegible] that in the ſeuenth number are compriſed all churches of [illegible] times throughout the whole worlde. We are all, ſo many [illegible] beleue, the children of one heauenly father: And therfore all ſpiritual brethren in Chriſt, coinheriters with Chriſt, and heires of God. Which thing S. Paul taught after Chriſt.[note 89] And ſeing our dignitie is ſo great, let vs ones be aſhamed of our miſdedes, leaſt our memory be put out of this moſte noble and celeſtiall familie. It is a ſhame the brother of Chriſt, of S. Iohn and all the Apoſtles ſhould degenerate. &c. But why haue not they ſo inſtantly vrged this brotherhood, as the Munkes haue beaten in their forged fraternities, the Roſaries of the virgin Mary and of Sainctes? Bycauſe that was fre, and coſt nothing: But the Monkes ſelle theirs dere. They be therfore diſceauers and ſeducers.

After he calleth him ſelfe partaker in affliction, or oppreſſion and perſecution, as he that was euen now banniſhed by the Emperour Domitian, and liued in exile. And he ioyneth together and not ſeparateth him ſelfe in the euill: That is co mon to all the faithfull brethren.[note 90] And verely it is one and the ſame perſecution, that vexed the Apoſtles, and tourmenteth vs at this day. Let vs therfore reioyce, that we haue the Apoſtles and all the Martyrs of Chriſt fellowes of our trouble and affliction, that we be broken and bruiſed with the heauy burthen of euils. Let vs therfore be paciet and long ſuffring. For it is not enough to be afflicted, and vexed with all kinde of euils (for many without any fruicte or prayſe at all indure moſte greuous paines.) But it becometh vs alſo to be patiet in aduerſitie. Therfore S. Iohn at this preſent ioyneth with all, patince. For the Lorde ſayd in the Goſpell. In your patience ſhall you poſſeſſe your ſoules.

After he addeth vnto tribulation and patience a kingdom, and that an heauenly not a terreſtrial kingdom.[note 91] And he bringeth in the kingdom for the comfort of the patient people. For alſo the Apoſtle S. Paul ſaid, a certen and ſure ſaying. For if we die with Chriſt, we ſhall lyue alſo with hym: If we ſuffer, we ſhall reygne with hym. &c. Let vs alwayes here with comfort our ſelues in aduerſitie. For we are thruſte downe, that we might ones be exalted againe ii. Corinthians iiii. And all theſe thinges are concluded in Chriſt Ieſus, by who we be both the children and brethren of God, and ſuffer many thinges patiently, and are made partakers of his kingdom. For euen for theſe thinges muſt we thanke him, and his m[illegible] rites, and not our own deſerte.

[note 92]Let vs here note alſo, what and howe great hath bene the humilitie of the greateſt and worthie Apoſtle of God, wh[illegible] was his ſtate: Not pleſaunt, but harde, yet in the pacience [illegible] Chriſt ioyfull. But where be they nowe, that glory in th[illegible] name of Apoſtles: Who in the meane time ſwellyng wit[illegible] pride, are addicte to filthie pleaſures? Whiche I warne, th[illegible] we flee from them, as from Apoſtataes.

[note 93]And now he ſheweth the place, where this diuine reuelation was made him, where alſo he was comaunded of Go[illegible] to wryte the ſame. The place was the Ile of Patmos. Th[illegible] ſame is accompted amonges the Ilondes called Sporad[illegible] of Plinie in the fourth boke and xii. cha. It lay ouer again[illegible] Aſia, and the citie of Epheſus, and was in thu ſight boeth [illegible] Europe and Affricke, ſo that it ſemed to be as it were a mi[illegible] dle ſeate, or holy chaire, out of the which Chriſt preached [illegible] Iohn from heauen to the whole worlde. And in dede the co[illegible] ſelles of God are wonderfull, and his goodnes is vnſpekeable, which reuealeth ſo great miſteries, as it were in th[illegible] Romiſh pryſon or Babilonicall captiuitie, to his faithfull.

[note 94]Neyther hideth he the cauſe of his comming into the ſam[illegible] Iland, I was there, ſaieth he, for the worde of God, and th[illegible] teſtimony of Ieſu Chriſt. The word of God is the very ſo[illegible] of God, called of Iohn by a ſingular proprietie of ſpeache the worde or ſermon of God, as appeareth the firſt of Iohn and the teſtimony of Ieſu Chriſt is the Goſpell if ſelf, whic[illegible] Ieſus teſtified, and the whiche his diſciples haue teſtified o[illegible] Ieſu. Therfore for the confeſſion and preaching of Ieſ[illegible] Chriſt, and of his holſome Goſpell (for ſo he expoundet[illegible] alſo how he is made partaker of thafflictio) Iohn was appr[illegible] hended in Aſia, and by ſoldiours led to Rome, that he might plea[illegible] his cauſe before themperour Domitian, who of his cruel nature condened the inuocet. And he was put into a cawdro o[illegible] hote boiling oyl. Out of the whiche when he eſcaped withou[illegible] harme, he was caried into Patmos. He aunſwered no other matter before themperour, than Paul did.27. yeres paſt, before Nero. This was done in that xiiii. or xv. yere of Domitia And the xxiiii. yeare after the deſtructio of the citie Hieruſale and after the birth of our Lorde lxvi. Domician who would ſeme and be called a God, being ſlaine of his own men, after many murthers and cruel actes, died himſelf a ſhameful death, the xv. yeare of his reigne. The authours hereof are, Suetonius in the life of Domitian, Tertullian in the heretic. preſcript. Euſebius in his chronicles, and in the third boke of the eccleſiaſtical hiſtory, in the xvii. and xviii. chapter. And hereto is added the common conſent of all writers.

Moreouer he noteth the tyme alſo,[note 95] in the whiche theſe miſteries began to be reuealed to him, [illegible], in that ſolemne day of the Lord, namely the ſonday. For ſo haue the auncient fathers called one of the ſabbothes, that is to ſay the firſt day in the weke, wherin Chriſt roſe again fro the dead. Math. xxviii. and Mark xvi. And this day haue the churches choſen to them ſelues in ſtead of the Sabboth day, as holy in the remebrauce of the Lordes reſurrectio, wherin they might kepe their ſacred and ſolemne aſſemblees. For that this day was ſolemniſed and coſecrated for aſſemblees in the congregation of Corinthe, appereth manifeſtly in the xvi. Chapter of the firſt Epiſtle to the Corintians, where the Apoſtle commaundeth to lay a part their collections in one of the ſabbothes: The ſame day alſo the faithful did celebrate their ſeruice with S. Paul, in the xx. of the Actes. Wher Sozomenus reporteth in the viii. chapt. of the firſt booke of the ſtory tripertite, that great Conſtantine made certen holy daies, and euen the Lordes daie for one, whiche is called of the Heathen the ſonday, it is to be vnderſtande that he renewed rather the cuſtome of the Apoſtles and catholique church, than to haue newly inſtituted the ſame. And frely of their own accord haue the churches receiued that day: for we read not that it was any where commaunded. And the congregations ſawe how it was altogether neceſſary that there ſhuld be a certen tyme, in the whiche the ſainctes ſhould mete and come together. They choſe therfore the day of the reſurrection, nether did they maliciouſly contende among theſelues for theſe thinges, as the hiſtories teſtifie was done in the churche afterward. And at this day verely, the ſuperſticious holy days being abrogated, it is better to obſerue certain and moderat daies, and to kepe peace and quietnes in the churche.

But where this Apoſtle knewe, that the faithfull on the ſo[illegible] day ſerued God in all aſſemblees, where he could not be p[illegible] ſent in body,[note 96] in ſpirit and contemplation he was with the[illegible] And as he was thus in the ſpirite and contemplation of m[illegible] ters diuine, and in holy prayers, he heard a voice, whereof [illegible] wyll ſpeake hereafter. But here we are preſently taugh[illegible] what is the religion of the ſonday, and how it is mete to o[illegible] ſerue it. Finally wordly men are reproued, whiche pollut[illegible] breake it with prophane workes and affaires. Dauid w[illegible] time he ſuffered perſecution of Saul, lamenteth chiefly, t[illegible] he might not come to the Lordes tabernacle. Our men a[illegible] compt it a great felicitie, neuer to enter into the felloſhip Sainctes. And to abuſe the ſonday, in gamenyng, drinkin[illegible] dauncing, and worldly buſineſſe.

[note 97]Theſe thinges on this wyſe declared, he cometh at len[illegible] to the reuelation: ſetting forth before the expreſſe commau[illegible] dement of God, wherby he was commaunded both to w[illegible] the thinges [illegible]euealed, and alſo to ſend the to the ſeuen church[illegible] of Aſia. To the maner and maieſtie of the reuelatio that ſa[illegible] chiefly apperteineth, that he heard a voice, and that notab[illegible] as the ſound of a trompet. For ſo we reade it was done the law geuing at the mount Sinay. Now is declared who voyce it was, and who was the authour of the reuelation Verely the eternall God, which calleth hym ſelfe Alpha [illegible] Omega, that is the beginning and the ende: Or as it is ſa[illegible] in Eſay, firſt and laſt. Wherof els where.

Now followeth the commaundement whiche hath [illegible] partes. For firſt the Lord commaundeth S. Iohn to w[illegible] And to wryte ſuche thinges as he ſawe, that is to witte, [illegible] Apocalipſe. And that he ſhould wryte nother in the ſan[illegible] nor on the walle, but in a boke: Verely for the edifiyng[illegible] profit of the churche preſent, and of all poſteritie. After he[illegible] alſo commaunded to ſende thoſe writinges to ſeuen cong[illegible] gations, and verely to all the churches of the whole world[illegible] al times and ages. Therfore al theſe thinges belong to the pro[illegible] of congregations, and that of al that be, haue bene or ſhal[illegible]

[note 98]Here of we learne, how great is the authoritie of the ſ[illegible] turs. It was not written nor copiled in bokes, but by Go[illegible] comaundment. There be notable teſtimonies of the bok[illegible] of Moyſes, in the xxxiiii. of Exod. and xxxi. of Deuter. And to ſay nothing of the reſidue of the Propetes, is not Ieremy commaunded to wryte his Sermons againe, whiche kyng Ioachim had cut in pieces and burnt?[note 99] Doubtes S. Peter beareth manifeſt witnes, that the Prophetes receiued the miſteries of God to none other ende, than thei ſhuld reueale them to vs: Which in dede might only be done by the ſcriptures. Now is Iohn moſte apertly commaunded to wryte. What wyl we ſay, that he is alſo commaunded to ſende his wrytinges to the congregations? Wherof againe we gather that God willeth right well to the congregations, and euen to euery one of vs. Let vs beware and take hede, that we put not from vs vnworthely ſo great benefites of God, to who be prayſe and glory.

## ¶ THE BEGINNING OF THE worke is made, and a moſte goodly deſcription to vs exhibited of Chriſte kyng and byſhop in glory, and neuertheles woorkyng in the Churche. The fifth Sermon.

ANd I tourned me, that I myght ſee the voice that ſpake with me. And when I was tourned, I ſawe ſeuen golden candelſtickes: And in the middes of the ſeuen candelſtickes, one like vnto the ſonne of man clothed with a linnen garmet [illegible]owne to the grounde, and gyrde about the pappes with a golden girdell. His head and his eares were whyte, as whyte wolle and [illegible]nowe: And his eyes were as a flame of fyre, [illegible]nd his feete like vnto braſſe, as though they brent in a fournace: and his voice as the ſound of many waters. And he had in his ryght and ſeuen Starres, and out of his mouthe went out a ſharpe two edged ſworde, and hi[illegible] face ſhone euen as the Sune in his ſtrength.

Suche thinges as haue bene treated of hitherto in th[illegible] boke be in ſtead of the prologue or preface, as they terme[illegible] Now at laſt ſhall the matter it ſelfe be propouded to vs.[note 100] H[illegible] therfore followeth the ſecond part of this boke, whiche r[illegible] cheth to the fourth chapter. In the which is Chriſt deſcribe vnto vs with his catholike churche. For firſt in dede is ſet [illegible] fore vs the moſte ſacred Image of Chriſt our Lord, teachi[illegible] what a one he is on the right hande of his father in glor[illegible] and how he ſittyng on the right hand of his father, worketh n[illegible] uertheleſſe in his churche continually, neuer abſent, preſe[illegible] alwayes. Of what ſorte moreouer the churche is here[illegible] earth, is figured in thoſe ſeuen congregations. Here therfo[illegible] are ſhewed the excellent giftes of churches, and agayne i[illegible] ſhamefull errours: How the Lord Chriſt confirmeth ſuch[illegible] are ſliding and ready to fall, eſtabliſheth thoſe that ſtande[illegible] forteth the weake harted, reſtreyneth the folyſhe hardy, a[illegible] preſerueth thinges that are corrupt: Finally how faithful [illegible] ſtours of the churche muſt worke and trauell, with the pe[illegible] ple committed to their credit. For here is exceadingly w[illegible] taught, what is the reparing and preſeruation of church[illegible] Where alſo a briefe ſomme of the whole eccleſiaſtical and [illegible] ſome doctrine brought in to an abridgement, ſhalbe ſet be[illegible] vs. For here is repeted from heauen of Chriſt in glory, [illegible] doctrine of true religion, whiche he had ſet forth more pl[illegible] tifully, when he was yet here in earth: And here moſt ap[illegible] applieth it to churches, after conſideration of the ſame.

And in moſt goodly order the wordes are knit togethe[illegible] (as likewyſe the whole boke is wrytten with playne wor[illegible] and hanging right well together, they are diſceaued that thi[illegible] it to be loſe beſomes or broomes vnbounde) Iohn heard voice behind him criyng: Wherupon he tourned backwa[illegible] that he might ſe the voyce ſpeaking, that is to wit, hym t[illegible] ſpake. For Aretas alſo admoniſſheth, that there is a trope[illegible] the wordes. For no man ſeeth, but heareth the voyce. A[illegible] tourning him to ſee, he ſawe a figure of Chriſt our ſauio[illegible] Therfore when the Lorde ſpeaketh, let vs tourne alſo w[illegible] all our harte,[note 101] that we may lykewyſe deſerue to ſe the miſteries of the kingdom of God, for he gladly reuealeth him ſel[illegible] to ſuche as tourne, and deſire heauenly thinges: And from thoſe that neglecte the miſteries of the kingdome of God, al thinges of ſaluation are hidde.

Further more S. Iohn exhibiteth to vs the Image of Chriſt, our catholike kyng and high biſhop ſitting in glory:[note 102] in the whiche deſcription are compriſed the chiefeſt matters of Chriſt. For ſuche a taſte of Chriſt is here geuen vs, as in this world may be of our weake fleſh perceiued. But we ſhal ſe hym at the length in the world to come ſuch as he is, in the fulnes of his maieſtie, wherin ſhalbe ioye and life euerlaſting: But this in this corrupt world, is yet graunted to no man.

So much therfore is permitted vnto vs that liue yet in thys worlde to be ſene, as is profitable,[note 103] and as our infirmitie may perceiue. But this ſame is not litle or nothing: but great and large and moſte full of ſpirituall pleaſure, I meane if we beholde theſe miſteries of God with a faithfull eye, and mynde deſyrous of Godly matters. And doubtles they be thynges certayn and true, that here are reuealed vnto vs. For they be reuealed by the very ſonne of God. Let vs not wyſh than to ſe more, or deſyre greater thinges than theſe are: But take pleaſure in thoſe which Chriſt hath graunted vs. And let vs knowe for certentie, that a wonderful benefite of God is geuen vs in this viſion. For who would not couet to ſe Chriſt in glory ſitting on the ryght hand of the father? Who deſyreth not to knowe what our Sauiour doth in heauen? And home being in heaue, is neuertheles preſent with his church in earth? But this ſacred and holy Image inſtructeth in all theſe poinctes all the faithfull of Chriſte moſte fully. Howebeit this Image of Chriſt is not to be ſet forth with colours, ſynce that colours can not atteine to the maieſtie therof: but with the eccleſiaſticall doctrine, whiche hath the promeſſe of the ſpirite of Chriſte: And is therfore more euident, and only mete for the true expreſſing therof. Let vs alſo prynt the ſame Image, not vpon any dead table with colours that wyll peryſhe and fade, but in our hartes through the liuely ſpirite of God, whiche may alſo kepe it in our myndes, neuer to be [illegible]wipt out. And ſuch thinges as are ſpoken in the ſeconde and third chap. Of this boke, are deriued of this deſcription[illegible] Chriſt, that the maieſtie of the thing might inuite vs to a ſ[illegible] gular diligence. The matter is very playne.

[note 104]Firſt we are taught who it is, whoſe Image is to vs ex[illegible] bited: Not the ſonne of man him ſelfe in his own ſubſtaun[illegible] but lyke the ſonne of man. The ſonne of man after the phr[illegible] of the Goſpell, is called Chriſt hym ſelfe very God and m[illegible] Here he ſhewed not hym ſelfe to be ſene of Iohn in his ou[illegible] ſubſtaunce, but in the fourme of an Aungell, that repreſent[illegible] Chriſt: Whiche thyng is oftener then once founde in th[illegible] boke. We ſhal therfore referre all theſe thinges vnto Chriſ[illegible] not to the Aungell, whiche is the miniſter of Chriſt in th[illegible] miſtery. And we ſhall ſee Chriſt in his owne ſubſtaun[illegible] what tyme our baſe body ſhall flitte from hence, and bey[illegible] reyſed from the dead ſhall be glorified. In the meane ty[illegible] the ſoull from the death of the body tyll it ryſe agayne, ſh[illegible] clearely haue the fruition of the ſight of Chriſt: Wherein, [illegible] I ſayd before ſhall be the chief ioye and felicitie. We ſh[illegible] nowe therfore ſee Chriſt as it were in a glaſſe, and ſo muc[illegible] as ſhall ſuffice vs. The Lorde open to vs the eyes of o[illegible] mynde.

[note 105]He telleth moreouer, where he ſawe Chriſt, in the midd[illegible] of ſeuen candelſtickes. By and by we ſhall perceiue that [illegible] the candelſtickes muſt be vnderſtande the churches. Chri[illegible] is than in the middes of the churche. He ſitteth verely on[illegible] ryght hand of the father, and after the proprietie of this [illegible] mane body, he is but in one place, and in no mo. As S. A[illegible] guſtin declareth aboundauntly in the lvii. Epiſtle to Dor[illegible] nus. Yet for as muche as he is alſo very God, he is lykew[illegible] in the myddes of the churche, as he promiſed in the Goſp[illegible] Wherſoeuer two or three be gathered in my name,[note 106] the[illegible] am in the myddes of them. And agayne: behold I am w[illegible] you vnto the worldes ende. Therfore by his power diui[illegible] Chriſt remayneth and worketh in the churche preſent, a[illegible] not abſent. (Leaue therfore to inquire, what Chriſt doth [illegible] the ryght hand of his father, whether he ſitte continually?) And he is verely in the myddes of the churches, fyxed to [illegible] place, but ſhewyng hym ſelfe indifferently to all egall as helpefull. For he neyther accepteth perſones, nor ſlepe[illegible] He is not paynted, he is not Idle, nothyng regardyng matters of the churche: But is chiefly and only attentiue to the ſaluation of the ſame. Suche a one he promyſed him ſelfe to be in the.14.15. and.16. of Iohn. And ſeyng Chriſt is in the myddes of the churche, what Vicar moreouer ſhall he haue? Shall he haue that ennemy whiche is directly againſt hym?[note 107] For a Vicar is in ſteade of one abſent: But Chriſt is in the middell of the churche, preſent, not abſent.

In the text followyng Chriſt is deſcribed moſte plentifully, many thynges are aſcribed vnto him: And is declared in what ſort Chriſt is in the mids of the church. And firſt indede is ſhewed, what garment he hath on: To wit both prieſtly and princely. By the which thing is figured, what maner of one Chriſt is in heauen and in earth: To wit biſhop and king, interceſſour, mediatour, and ſacrifice, a moſte perfit ſanctification and iuſtification, a redemer and deliuerer of the faithful to his father, euermore working the ſaluation of his faithfull. As S. Paul teacheth, Roma. 8. Hebr. 7. Poderes[note 108] is found amogſt the apparell of Aaron, and it is a prieſtly garment. Whereof S. Hierome writeth to Fabiola, of the prieſtly garmet. The ſecond veſture of linnen is a coate downe to the fote, of double launde: Which Ioſephus calleth Biſsina. And it is called in Hebrew ketheneth, in Greke, [illegible]. This cleaueth iuſt to the body, and is ſo narrowe and ſtrayte ſleued, that there is no wryncle at al in the garment, and came downe to the legges. This was verely whyte and cleane.[note 109] For the Lorde Chriſt is an vndefiled Prieſt. Hebre. 7. Neyther weareth he agayne a foule veſture, as he did. Zachar. 3. Nor a purple, as in the.19. of Iohn. But a bright one, as he that hath obteyned a name aboue all names. But his gyrdell or belte is worne of Souldiours and triumphaunt perſones: And it ſignifieth in Chriſt the dignitie roiall. For Chriſt is king, delyuerer and redemer of the faythfull. His victory is ours. He hath ouercome Sathan, Helle, ſynne and death.

But the belte or girdle of Chriſte is not ſet in the wonted place, to wytte, about the loynes. For as Aretas hath alſo admoniſhed, ther ar no cocupiſcences to be reſtrained in Chriſt.
