Therefore is he not gyrded after the maner of ſynners, but about the pappes or breſtes: to thintent we ſhould vnderſtande by the girding, that he is kyng of kynges, voyd[illegible] of all affections: Moſte rightuous and holy in iudgements and gouernement: But yet in the meane tyme furnyſhed fo[illegible] the defence of his church, as we haue red it written in the.9[illegible] Pſalme. The Lord hath put on ſtrength and girded hymſelf, &c. Chriſt might ſeme to haue girded him ſelfe not after the maner that prieſtes or kynges vſe, for that he hath obteyned [illegible] more excellent prieſthod and kyngdome, induring for eue[illegible] To accompliſhe theſe thinges it behoued hym to vſe a te[illegible] ple, and palace not tranſitory, but heauen it ſelfe. Hebre. vi[illegible] and ix. Yet in the meane tyme the effect pearſeth in to th[illegible] church it ſelfe, that ſo he may be preſent in the church alſo.

[note 110]But the head of Chriſt appeareth hoare, and his hea[illegible] whyte, lyke moſte pure wolle, and whyteſt ſnow. Suche a head is aſcribed alſo to the father of our Lord Ieſu Chriſt, [illegible] the ſeuenth Chap. of Daniel. For they be of the ſame eſſen[illegible] And hereby is ſignified wyſdome and age, and alſo the e[illegible] nitie and deitie of Chriſt. And by reaſon that Chriſt is Gi[illegible] therfore is he head of the church, miniſtring to the body li[illegible] ſpirite, wyſdome, and all giftes celeſtiall. Epheſ. v. Ca[illegible] Pope of Rome, that moſte wicked man of ſynne doe th[illegible] What an head is he than? without lyfe, without brayne[illegible] moſte folyſhe. As he is deſcribed in the xi. of Zacha. And is a ſhame, that we will not ſe theſe thinges, being blynd euer. Chriſt is euerlaſting, omnipotent, and that knoweth thing: And he may be the health and head of the body. I[illegible] in the beginning, ſaith he, was the word, and the word [illegible] with God. &c. Chriſt him ſelf: before Abraham was, ſaith [illegible] I am. Therfore the Heretickes lye, deniyng Chriſt to be[illegible] ry God, of the ſame ſubſtauce with the father. He is the [illegible] dome of God, all thinges are ſubiect to hym. Ephe. the [illegible] And he him ſelf fulfilleth all thinge, after the ſame ſort be[illegible] preſent with his churche.

[note 111]Now are his eyes alſo not darkened nor blynde, but fy[illegible] and bryght. For Chriſt knoweth all thyng. Chriſtes eye[illegible] watchful, nothyng is hyd from him, he ſeeth all thinges [illegible] are done, both good and euyll. And he ſeeth to the intent may iudge and require. He is light in darknes, and the ſi[illegible] of Chriſt is to good men ioyfull in perils. Finally the iudgementes of Chriſt are rightuous. The Prophet Dauid: The eyes of the Lord, ſayth he, vpon the iuſt, and his eares are to their prayer. Agayne: The face of the Lorde is on them that do euyll. And like as the head is not plucked from the body, ſo Chriſt can not be abſent from his churche. And ſeyng that his eyes are quicke ſighted, and that the Lord forſeeth al our thinges, and hath the charge ouer vs, how is he abſent from his churche? What nedeth there any vicar?

And the fete of the Lorde are of copper,[note 112] or lyke vnto braſſe and frankenſence burning in a fornace. For Chalcolibaum, is a worde compounded of braſſe and frankenſence.[note 113] This noteth Eraſmus, and that Swidas ſheweth alſo the ſame, that there is a kynde of Copper more precious than gold: which he ſayth is made of Salt peter and of a ſtone. Plin. in the xxxiiii. and ii. Chapter. calleth it a kynde of braſſe, whiche is digged out of the vaynes of the earth, in tymes paſte had in price. It ſhould ſeme to me to be the ſame, whiche in the firſt and tenth of Ezechiel is called Haſmal, a preſent remedy againſt poyſons. For if wyne intoxicated be put into a cuppe therof, it wil hiſſe. And ſo is the death and poyſon detected.

The moſte cleane braſen and firy fete, do ſignifie the conuerſation, and the wayes of the Lorde blameles, his iudgements right and iuſt. And that he ſo walketh in the church, and gouerneth al things, that in the meane ſeaſon al vncleanes be detec [illegible]ed and conſumed, but he him ſelfe remaineth always moſte holy and pure. For fire pourgeth. God is a conſuming fire.

But the voyce of Chriſt,[note 114] is as it were the noyſe of many waters, not ſo muche for that all nations and people do commende and prayſe him: But for as muche as the Goſpel and worde of God, came into the whole worlde. Whiche voyce alſo moſte mightie kinges could leſſe aſſwage and appeaſe, than they could do the guſſhing of waters,[note 115] or to ſtoppe the wyndes in ſackes. Therfore by the power of preaching the Lorde is always preſent in his churche.

The hand is an inſtrument of all inſtrumentes, eſpecially the righthande.[note 116] In this Chriſt holdeth ſeuen ſtarres, to wit ſeuen prelates or paſtours of churches in Aſia: And euen all the byſhops throughout the whole world Chriſt by his power geueth to vs as Paſtours, and inſtructeth, comforte[illegible] confirmeth and defendeth them, to the end they ſhould preac[illegible] his worde. Wherby he may ioyne hym ſelfe to the churc[illegible] Chriſt worketh by them in the churche and preſerueth th[illegible]

[note 117]The ſame is more lyuely expreſſed in the wordes that f[illegible] lowe. For a ſharpe two edged ſworde commeth out of [illegible] Lordes mouth. This ſwearde, is the worde of God, as [illegible] ryght well declared in the ſixt to the Epheſ. and fourth to [illegible] Hebrewes. And this word or ſworde hangeth not vpon [illegible] walle nor ſticketh faſt in the ſheathe, nor hangeth by the f[illegible] but cometh out of the mouth. He ſayth not, it came foort[illegible] it ſhall come forth: But it cometh forth, as the thyng th[illegible] in continuall operation, or perpetuall preaching through[illegible] the worlde. And it is two edged, ſharpe and pearſing, aſ[illegible] in the heart of the Godly vnto ſaluation, as in the heart[illegible] the wycked to payne and condemnation. And yet at [illegible] day cometh out that ſworde of the mouthe of Chriſt by mouthes of Miniſters. The worde of Chriſte is in dede [illegible] temned of the world, and is called of many a fable: But [illegible] a ſworde, and that a ſworde out of Chriſtes mouth. All [illegible] vnfaithfull do finde and ſhall fynde this, howe ſoeuer [illegible] reſiſte. With this ſworde Chriſt kylleth the wicked. And effect of this ſworde is greater than was the ſworde of A[illegible] ander, Pompey, Iulius Ceſar, or Marius, Attille, or [illegible] merlane. Neyther maketh it any ſkylle, though the w[illegible] now acknowledge it not. It ſhall doe in tyme to com[illegible] their greateſt euyll. Doubtles with this ſpirite of his m[illegible] the Lorde continueth alwayes to comfort and gouern[illegible] churche, ſo that he is neuer abſent from the ſame.

Finally the countenaunce of Chriſt ſhineth as the [illegible] doeth in his greateſt ſtrength about none, when it is [illegible] bryght, clere, and pleſaunt, by the countenaunce we [illegible] men chiefly. Therfore by the countenauce we know C[illegible] The countenaunce of Chriſt is light. Chriſt therfore is [illegible] And that verely a diuine and eternall lyght, lyghtyng a[illegible] that they may alſo be made the children of light: and tha[illegible] faces of ſaintes may ſhine in that day, as bright as the ſi[illegible] and as the face of Chriſt ſhone. Math. 13. and.17. And f[illegible] he doeth thus communicate this lyght vnto vs. (Iohn [illegible] and.1. Iohn. 1.) How is it to be thought that Chriſt ſhould be abſent from his churche? Thou ſeeſt how he is preſent.

And ſo hath our Lord Chriſt exhibited hym ſelfe to vs to be ſene vnto ſaluation, and hath opened hym ſelfe wholy vnto vs, as he is, what he doeth for vs, and howe he is in hys churche. In theſe thinges are all the miſteries of the Goſpell comprehended. For what can you ſay of Chriſt, that you haue not herein compryſed. Let vs therfore remembre them. and wryte them in our myndes, that we may imbrace Chriſt kyng and byſhop, and that we neuer let hym departe out of our armes. To hym be glory.

## ¶ How Iohn was affected towardes the viſion to hym exhibited, the comfort of Iohn, and the expoſition of the viſion, applied vnto conſolation. The ſixt Sermon.

ANd when I ſawe hym I fel at his fete euen as dead: And he layd his right hand vpon me, ſaying vnto me: Feare not, I am the firſt and the laſte, and am alyue, and was [illegible]ead. And beholde I am alyue for euer more, [illegible]nd haue the keyes of hel, and of death, write [illegible]herfore the thinges whiche thou haſte ſene, [illegible]nd the thinges whiche are, and the thinges whiche ſhalbe fulfilled hereafter: And the miſtery of the ſeuen Starres which thou ſaweſt in my right hand, and the ſeuen golden candelſtickes. The ſeuen Starres are the meſſengers of the ſeuen congregations: And [illegible]he ſeuen candelſtickes whiche thou ſaweſt, [illegible]re the ſeuen congregations.

It followeth how bleſſed S. Iohn was moued with that celeſtiall and wonderfull viſion: And how he receiued conſolation, more ouer the expoſition of the viſion applied to his comfort, with a commaundement to indite all theſe thing[illegible] diligently.

[note 118]What tyme he had fully ſene this diuine and heauenly ſight of Chriſt our Lord, ſitting on the right hand of God i[illegible] glory, his ſtrength failing him, he falleth down on the earth: and liyng at the fete of the Lorde, is lyke a dead body. W[illegible] reade that the ſame chaunced to Daniel in the x. Chapt. And other men of God alſo haue bene feared with the viſions o[illegible] Aungels. The women alſo in the new Teſtament tremble[illegible] at the ſepulchre, ſeing Aungels. Peter was amaſed at th[illegible] greatnes of the miracle. Luke v. And falling at the knees [illegible] the Lord, crieth out, go from me, Lord, for I am a ſynful m[illegible] For Godly viſions bewray our infirmitie:[note 119] Neyther be [illegible] apte or ſufficiently pourged, to behold thoſe ſuperceleſt[illegible] thinges with eyes and myndes ſicke and not yet well pu[illegible] fied. Therfore muſt the elect be glorified in an other lyfe, th[illegible] they may be made partakers of the glory celeſtiall. In th[illegible] meane ſeaſon here al godly are humbled and abaſed by hyg[illegible] viſions and reuelations.[note 120] For they auaunce not them ſelue[illegible] proudly through the glory of reuelatio: But perceiuing th[illegible] naturall corruption, they craue pardon, and the augmentation of the ſuperceleſtiall grace and light. For vnleſſe we b[illegible] illumined with the ſpirite of God, we ſhal lye like dead folk[illegible] how ſo euer we receyue with our corporall ſenſes the viſio[illegible] celeſtiall.

[note 121]But they that humble them ſelues before the Lorde, fynd[illegible] a moſte preſent conſolation at the Lordes hande. Wherfo[illegible] there came to S. Iohn immediatly both in worde and de[illegible] a full conſolation. For the Aungell repreſenting the perſon[illegible] of Chriſt, layeth his right hand vpon Iohn: The whiche is token of amitie, protection, and of preſent helpe. For in [illegible] preſſing this maner of ſpeaking in dutche, we ſay therfore [illegible] laiyng on the hand is ſignified, that Chriſt is good to Iohn, ready to helpe him. Which incontinently he maketh play[illegible] by the addition of wordes, ſaiyng, feare not.[note 122] Whiche ſaiy[illegible] is common euery where in the ſtory of the Goſpel: And th[illegible] fore is moſt goſpel like, that is to ſaye moſt lucky. For God commaundeth the humbled to be of good hope, and to liue aſſured vnder the protectio of the higheſt. Which verely we vnderſtand to be ſpoken not to Iohn alone but to al vs alſo, that we in like maner, albeit that we fele the infirmitie of our fleſhe, ſhould yet hope well of the goodnes and mercy of God. Here followeth that cauſe more fully declared, why Iohn ſhold not be affrayed. For the viſion ſhewed was not exhibited for the terrour of him: but that Iohn might perceiue how great and mightie he is which is prepared for the defence of him and al the faithfull. As though he ſhoulde ſaye. Where thou ſeeſt how great he is, which hath taken vpo him to defende thee, who finally protecteth and gouerneth the whole Churche, there is no cauſe why thou ſhouldeſt be affraide. But rather execute boldely, that he comaundeth thee. Wryte that he co maundeth to be written. Be not affrayed of men, feare God rather. For if good me be ſo ſore afrayde at the ſight of him, where ſhal the enemies and contemners of God appere?

Therfore conſequently he expoudeth the viſion, teaching who he is, which was ſene like to the ſonne of man walking emonges the golden Candelſtickes. And he applieth this expoſitio vnto comforte: that both Iohn and euery faithful maye perceiue, how mightie Chriſt is, and what the faithfull haue by him obteined. For the Aungell tempereth his ſpeach ſo, that we maie ſeme to heare al things ſpoke to vs not by the mouth of the Aungell, but of Chriſt him ſelfe.[note 123] And this expoſition hath his partes. For firſt he declareth (as I ſaid euen nowe) whoſe Image it was that was ſhewed. Than is annexed a comaundement to write this boke. After that is opened the miſterie of the ſtarres. Finally the ſecretnes of the cadelſticks is reuealed: And al theſe thinges right plainly and briefly.

Firſt thou haſt ſene, ſaieth the Lord, a viſion,[note 124] wherat thou waſte amaſed: but feare thou not. For thou haſt not ſene any euill or fearful ſpirite, boding any miſfortune: but my ſhape, whiche am thy redemer and Lorde. I am firſt and laſte. And this maner of ſpeakyng (as I warned a little before) he toke out of the Prophecies of Eſaye, as it is to be ſeene in the xli. xliiii. xlv. and xlviii. chapters. And he ſignifieth him ſelfe to be coequall, and of the ſame ſubſtaunce with the father in all thinges, very God, eternall, and incoprehenſible. For loke what thinges the father attributeth to himſelf, the ſame alſo doth the ſonne vſurpe. But there is no order or time certain to be vnderſtade in firſt and laſt, but plainly euerlaſtingnes. Therfore Chriſt here ſignifieth,[note 125] that he is very God, egalle and of the ſame eſſence with the father from all eternitie. As the ſame is alſo muche confirmed in Iohn 1.5.10.14. and 17. chapter. This fighteth againſt the Heretickes, which at that time alſo, as at this daye the Seruetanes, denie the eternall deitie of Chriſt the Lorde. And thus, when the trewe God is of vs acknoweledged and beleued, he maye be for our Saluation. Yf Chriſte be not very God, he is not our ſaluation. For I am God, ſayeth the veritie: And beſides me there is no God, no Saluation.

Secondly he ſayeth, I am liuing, and was dead: wherby he ſignifieth that he toke the true humane nature. The whiche many alſo at the ſame time denied: In like caſe as the[illegible] be ſome at this daye, whiche do playnely derogate from th[illegible] humanitie of Chriſte. Agaynſte all ſuche maner of hereſies the Lorde him ſelfe confeſſeth that he was dead. Wherby it is now manifeſt, that he is very ma, as he is alſo very God, of the ſame eſſece with his father in deitie, as he is alſo of the ſame ſubſtaunce with vs in humanitie, like vnto vs in all thinges, ſinne excepted. For he toke not the nature of Aungelles, but the ſede of Abraham. And it behoued in dede tha[illegible] the ſonne of man ſhuld be incarnate, that bothe he might di[illegible] and ſhede bloud.[note 126] For the Teſtament in the dead is finally ratified neither is there any remiſſion made without bloud ſhe[illegible] ding. The Lord therfore dieth, and ſhedeth bloud, to the int[illegible] he might geue full remiſſio of ſinnes, and confirme the new[illegible] Teſtament. Yet euen he that was thought to be dead, nowe liueth,[note 127] and is that ſame liuing, who hauing vaquiſhed death, the iii. daye roſe againe from the dead, and repared life for a[illegible] beleuers, and inſpireth into them his owne very life.

And therefore addeth immediatly: beholde I am liuing[illegible] world without ende. For nowe Chriſt dieth no more, death[illegible] ſhal not rule ouer him. But rather he is the life of al his fait[illegible] full, who in riſing againe, brought agayne life: and that life euerlaſtinge, induringe I ſaye worlde without ende. As he him ſelfe declareth more at large. Iohn. 5.6.10. chapter. And thapoſtle to the Rom. 4.1. Corinth. 15. and 2. Timoth. 1.

Moreouer where many were wote to doubte of this life gotten and repared by Chriſte, the Lorde him ſelfe confirmeth, that he ſaied, by an othe, and ſayeth. Amen. As though he ſhould ſaye, this is altogether trewe and vndoubted that I ſaye.

Finally he addeth,[note 128] and I haue the keyes of Hell and of death. By the whiche woordes againe he comforteth exceadingly, and expreſſeth his power: and declareth howe great he is, and what we haue of him. Here muſt we ſpeake by the waie of the kepe. The ordinary gloſe ſaieth very wel: he that hath the keyes of any houſe, ſayeth he, letteth in whome he wil, and kepeth backe whom he will from entring in. Therfore Chriſt poſſeſſeth the keyes of death and hel, for that whom he will, he deliuereth from perpetual codemnation of death: And whom he wil,[note 129] he ſuffereth to remaine iuſtely in the ſame daunger of damnation. And verely Eſaye in the 22. ſpeaking of Eliachim, whome he ſayeth ſhoulde be made Iudge in the courte of Ezechias: I wil laye ſayeth he the keye of the houſe of Dauid vpon his ſhulder: whiche ſhal open, and no manne ſhall ſhutte: ſhall ſhutte, and no man ſhal open. Therfore are the keyes put in the Scripture for the charge and gouernement of the houſe. Eliachim ſhall gouerne all thinge in the Courte of Ezechias vprightly. What ſoeuer he ſhall determine, no manne ſhal infringe, that whiche he ſhall abrogate, no manne ſhall reſtore. Chriſte therfore, a figure of whome Eliachim repreſented, ſhal him ſelfe haue alſo the chiefe gouernment in the houſe or kingdome of God: ſo that whom he will, he may quicke, and plucke backe from hel and from damnation: And againe whom he liſte to condemne, he may diſtroie by his iuſte iudgement. For he hath moſt ful power ouer death and hell.[note 130] For bothe two hath he ouercomen and made weake. And theſe thinges comforte the faithfull moſte ſtrongely, and reteyne them in all Godly duties. And that ſame is chiefly to be obſerued, that he ſayeth not, he had the keyes, or ſhall haue, but I haue, ſayeth he, I haue I ſaye. He gaue not his power to the Biſhoppe of Rome, but hath i[illegible] him ſelfe, and will kepe it ſtill for euer. And he gaue not to the Apoſtles ful power of life and death, of ſaluation and damnation,[note 131] and ſo vnarmed him ſelfe: but he gaue the keyes of opening and ſhutting heauen, as it were to his Miniſters and ſeruitours, by the preaching of the Goſpell: by the whiche he promiſed life to all that beleued, Chriſt him ſelfe ſhoulde geue that life for the truth of the promiſe: To whom ſo euer they ſhould threaten damnation, Chriſt him ſelfe ſhuld condemne for the truth of his woorde. We ſee therfore that the Lord kepeth ſtil and exerciſeth the power, and his miniſters the miniſterie (by preaching, not by abſolute power.) Therfore the Pope is Antichriſt,[note 132] which vſurpeth and taketh vpon him this full power and authoritie in Heauen and in Earth, and in the middes of the earth alſo, or beyonde all the earth, i[illegible] thoſe vnfortunate Ilandes. I meane purgatory. By the whic[illegible] craftie deuiſe he hath ſubtilly emptied the purſes, Coffer[illegible], garuers and wine cellers,[note 133] of fooliſh people that ſwarne from the articles of their beliefe, to wit I beleue the forgeuenes o[illegible] ſinnes, the reſurrection of the fleſhe, and life euerlaſting. Th[illegible] beaſte dare vſurpe the two hornes of the Lambe,[note 134] namely th[illegible] authoritie of King and Biſhop, and therfore to hange two keye[illegible] vnder his triple Crowne: that euen by theſe Armes all the world maye perceyue, that this is very he, whiche, hauin[illegible] ſubdued three kinges or hornes, is crope vp, and chalengeth t[illegible] him ſelfe all power in heauen and in earth, ſignified by the tw[illegible] keies. And ſurely the blindnes of our time is wonderful an[illegible] to be lameted, that hauing eyes it ſeeth yet nothing. Let ſuc[illegible] as be wiſe, remeber, that Chriſte hath yet the keyes of deat[illegible] and Hell, his miniſters the denouncyng of life and death.

[note 135]And nowe when he had declared theſe greate and moſ[illegible] holeſome matters, and had comforted the minde of Iohn, h[illegible] addeth the commaundement: write the viſion exhibited, f[illegible] nally write thoſe thinges alſo whiche muſte be done ſhorte[illegible] after this. He placeth in the middes, and thoſe that be: th[illegible] is, whiche are in dede, and trewe, and be not falſe. And theſ[illegible] things are to get authoritie to this boke, finally to the who[illegible] ſcripture, whiche is reuealed with like truth of the ſelfe ſam[illegible] Author. And as Iohn is comaunded to write without fear[illegible] ſo are we comaunded to Preache and publiſh the ſame boldly though the world be neuer ſo madde therat.

He addeth moreouer, the expoſition that remaineth, and ſayeth: The miſterie of the ſeuen ſtarres, &c. The reaſon ſemeth almoſt vnperſit. Therfore muſte we vnderſtande this is the miſtery or ſacrament of the ſeuen Starres and Candelſtickes: that it maye be as it were a propoſition,[note 136] and that the expoſition ſhoulde folow immediatly: the ſeuen ſtarres, are ſeuen Meſſengers, &c. And by Sacrament vnderſtande a ſecret miſtery, and the very expoſition of the miſtery. As yf you woulde ſ[illegible] here is to be noted the goodnes of Almightie God,[note 137] whiche declareth to vs him ſelfe the hardeſt places of the Scripture. Where be they therfore that accuſe the Scripture of obſcurenes, and contende that it can not be vnderſtade? let vs here marke alſo the comon maner of ſpeakyng of the whole ſcripture: ſeuen ſtarres,[note 138] be ſeuen Meſſengers: The ſeuen lightes, are ſeuen Churches. For ſignes receyue the names of the thinges, although they be, and remaine in theyr owne ſubſtaunce, and bee not chaunged into another. This the very contentiouſe perſons do graunte alſo, whiche in the woordes of the ſupper, this is my body, will acknowledge no figuratiue ſpeache at all.

Starres be called Aungelles. Aungelles be Gods Meſſengers, paſtours of churches, ſo called in the ii. and iii. chapter of Malachie. For God ſendeth preachers as Ambaſſadors to the people, and willeth them to be hearde in like caſe as himſelfe. Luke x. Iohn xiii. Let no man therfore tary till the Lord him ſelfe come downe from heauen againe, and preach [illegible]nto vs. Euen now he preacheth to vs by his Meſſengers, whiche preache his, that is to ſaye the woorde of Chriſt, yf [illegible]ou contemne them, you contemne Chriſt. Preachers be [illegible]alled ſtarres, by reaſon of theyr bright and Heauenly doc [illegible]rine, and for their purenes of life.[note 139] Beware therefore you Preachers, that you be not wandering Planettes, leeſt the [illegible]aue no light at all, neyther in doctrine, nor conuerſation of [illegible]fe. For than the ſhall be likened to ſtarres that fall downe [illegible]ut of Heauen, as ſhall happen here after in this boke to the [illegible]alſe teachers.

But thoſe ſtarres are not in the head, or in the fete,[note 140] or on [illegible]he backe or ſides: but in the right hande of Chriſt. Whiche [illegible]ing hath in dede a great conſolation for the Paſtours be in the right hande of God, in Gods protection, neither ſh[illegible] any man take them out of his hande. God him ſelfe alſo [illegible] ueth paſtours, and furniſheth them with neceſſarie good[illegible] of the Church. Therfore is the whole gouernement and gl[illegible] ry his. Wherefore the Apoſtle ſayeth alſo: he that water[illegible] and planteth is nothing, but God that geueth increaſe.

Nowe as concernyng the Candelſtickes, there was o[illegible] verely in the Tabernacle of Moſes with ſeuen ſockettes,[note 141] [illegible] ſet in ſeuen Candelles. In Salomons temple were ten ca[illegible] delſtickes. The one repreſented a figure of Chriſt: And the [illegible] uen therupon, and the ten, betokened the vniuerſalitie[illegible] Churches, whiche are lighted all of the only lighte Chriſ[illegible] and haue of this one, what light ſo euer they haue. And th[illegible] candelſtickes are of Golde. The miſtery whereof Aretas e[illegible] pounding: They are all golde, ſayeth he, for the puritie a[illegible] preciouſnes of faith liyng hidde in them. And in dede the [illegible] delſtickes of them ſelues geue no light, but be receptacles [illegible] light. So of vs arriſeth no light, but darkenes. But in [illegible] that light euerlaſting, ſet a light in the candleſticke, the lig[illegible] ſhineth: if Chriſte illumine the Churche with faithe and [illegible] retie, than faith ſheweth foorth her ſelfe in open confeſſio[illegible] and the purenes of life in conuerſation. And this the Lord [illegible] quireth of his churche in the v. chapter of Matth. So let yo[illegible] light ſhine &c. And the apoſtle in the ii. to the Philipp. In t[illegible] middes of a frowarde and croked natio ſhine like lightes the worlde.

And hitherto we haue handled the conſolation of Chri[illegible] and the expoſition of that great and celeſtiall viſion, whe[illegible] we haue learned the miſteries of the faith of Chriſt and of his Churche: to the ende we ſhould knowe that Chriſt is the Lorde reignyng in his Church, and applying al thinges to the ſaluation of his faythfull. That he ſendeth Preachers, teacheth by them, and kepeth and defendeth them. To him be Glory &c.

## ¶ Of the Epiſtles reuealed out of the throne of God from Chriſt by an Aungell, and receiued and ſent of Iohn. Where alſo a parte of the Epiſtle to the Epheſians is expounded. The ſeuenth Sermon.

VNto the Meſſenger of the congregation of Epheſus write: Theſe things ſaieth he that holdeth the ſeuen Starres in his righthand, and walketh in the middes of the Seuen golden Candelſtickes. I knowe thy workes, and thy labour and thy patiece, and [illegible]ow thou canneſt not forbeare them whiche [illegible]re euil: And examineſt them which ſaie thei are Apoſtles, and are not: And haſte founde [illegible]hem liars: and haſt ſuffered, and haſt patience: [illegible]nd for my names ſake haſte laboured, and [illegible]aſte not fainted. Neuertheleſſe I haue ſome what againſte thee, becauſe thou haſte lefte [illegible]hy firſte loue.

Your charitie hath ſene a certen Image of the lord chriſt,[note 142] [illegible]itting on the right hande of the father in glorye: yet ſo, that [illegible] no wiſe he eyther forſaketh or neglecteth his Churche. Now followeth it more fully and plainely howe our Saui [illegible]ur Chriſte in Heauen executeth the office of the highe Biſhop, and teacheth the whole churche by his miniſters, rebu [illegible]eth, comforteth, and reteyneth it in her dutie: Finally tour [illegible]eth alwayes awaye thinges hurtefull, and auaunceth it to greater thinges. For here follow ſeuen Epiſtles, to the ſeue Congregations: that is to witte,[note 143] vnto all the churches in the [illegible]hole worlde. For this moſte ample and holſome doctrine [illegible]aye not be reſtreyned to a fewe, ſins Chriſt is Biſſhoppe [illegible]niuerſall.[note 144]

But great is the autoritie of theſe epiſtles. For they are reuealed from the throne of God, by the ſonne of Go[illegible] ſpeaking by an Aungell, whiche preſcribeth what is to b[illegible] written in thoſe Epiſtles: S. Iohn receiueth and writeth th[illegible] ſame, through Chriſt his comaundement, and ſendeth the[illegible] to the ſeuen congregations: And verely they aperteyne n[illegible] leſſe vnto vs, than if now the bearer entring into the churc[illegible] ſhould deliuer theſe letters vnto vs.

Moreouer in theſe ſeuen Churches is figured vnto v[illegible] the nature,[note 145] maners, vices, medicines, rebukes, prayſes of [illegible] Churches in all times, and what ſoeuer is wonte to chaun[illegible] aboute them. Then by examples of moſte excellent mean[illegible] mixed, of Hypocriticall alſo and wicked. And theſe our Lo[illegible] dothe euidetly inſtructe, reproue, rebuke, and blame, praiſ[illegible] correct, moue, exhorte, comforte, the ſame he threatneth, an[illegible] promiſeth them alſo ioyfull thinges, &c. This is no light [illegible] comon example, but of the ſonne of God, the high and mo[illegible] bleſſed Biſhop: teaching vs, howe we ſhould deale with [illegible] congregations after the capacitie and diſpoſition of euery on[illegible]

And not without cauſe he chouſeth vnto him ſeuen th[illegible] moſt noble cities of Aſia. Certaine it is, that Aſia was of [illegible] firſt inhabited, and from thence they were diſperſed into [illegible] ther partes of the worlde. Certaine it is alſo, that the Deu[illegible] ſet vp his Throne in Aſia, and there reigned in men throug[illegible] Idolatrie, murther, ambition, auarice, vncleanes, and filth[illegible] pleaſures. For the prouerbe is knowe, the laughter Iouica[illegible] It is knowen what the apoſtle wrote to the Epheſias in th[illegible] iiii. and v. chapter. Therfore wold our Sauiour Chriſt oue[illegible] throwe that Throne of the deuill, and ſet vp that his Thron[illegible] of rightuouſneſſe and holines. Therefore goeth he firſ[illegible] and chiefly to them of Aſia, that by their example the whol[illegible] worlde might be corrected and amended.

[note 146]And amonges other cities of Aſia and Iouia, Epheſu[illegible] was moſt famouſe, called in the olde time the lighte of Aſi[illegible] And amonges the xii. cities of Iouia accompted the princ[illegible] pall.[note 147] Neyther was there any other richer or more beautifu[illegible] Church ſene any where in Aſia, than the temple of Diana [illegible] Epheſus. It ſtode in the middes of the citie, a great wond[illegible] of the Greke magnificence, as writeth Plinie. This temp[illegible] is ſayed to haue ben two hundreth and twentie yeres in m[illegible] king of al Aſia: And ſet in a fenny ground, that it ſhould fele of no earth quakes nor opening of the earthe. The length of [illegible]t was ccccxxv. foote, the breadth ccxx. It had pillers an ho dreth twentie and ſeuen, dedicated of ſo many kinges. Seke the reſte oute of the epitome of the famouſe D. Ioachim Vadiane. The apoſtle S. Paul firſte illumined this citie with the [illegible]eame of the Goſpel. Whoſe epiſtle to the ſame citie remayneth, and a plentifull ſtory in the actes of the Apoſtles. After Paull was executed, Iohn went to Epheſus,[note 148] and from thece preached to al Aſia: from thece alſo was he brought to Rome [illegible]o the emperour Domitian: To Epheſus he retourned after his exile: and there at length as the eccleſiaſticall ſtories do teſtifie, he ſlepte in the Lord.

And before euery epiſtle, muche more to that of the Epheſians, is ſet a commaundement, write.[note 149] This comaundement geueth authoritie to the writynge, ſo that we maye not axe, whether this writing, ought to be credited? and whie it ſhuld [illegible]e beleued? For here is the expres comaundement of God, [illegible]nd the diuine authoritie, whereof curiouſely to inquire is [illegible]hought not without cauſe vnlawfull. Moſes wrote by the [illegible]ommaundement of God. And by the ſame comaundement of God wrote alſo the prophetes and Apoſtles. Whic than are not theyr wrytinges beleued to be Anthenticall? Certes Iohn ſayed truely and wittely: he that knoweth God, hea [illegible]eth vs: he that is not of God, heareth vs not.1. Iohn.5. Curiouſe queſtios ceaſe, where the minde of the godly, or of any poore ſhepe knoweth the voice of his Lorde and Sheperde.

And let no man thinke, that this epiſtle beyng written to one Aungel, that is to a Biſhop or paſtour, apperteineth nothing to the Churche. For to the ende of the epiſtle is added an acclamation. He that hath an eare let him heare what the ſpirite ſayeth to the congregations. Therefore the paſtour is named, but the Shepe are not excluded. All degrees and ſtates in the church know, what is ſayd vnto them. Ignorauce ſaieth: That which is writte to the Romanes, cocerneth me nothing. Yet neuertheles it is intitled to the Aungel,[note 150] to the intet they paſtours may be admoniſhed, what the ſtate of the Church is.

The arg[illegible]ment of the firſte epiſtle is thus. Chriſt declareth that he ruleth ouer his church, that he taketh charge and gouernement of the ſame. Some thinge therin he prayſeth and ſomewhat he blameth: In the meane time he exhorteth to repentauce, threatening greuouſe thinges, and promiſing moſt ioyefull. And alſo he applieth this epiſtle to al churches and comunicateth it to all cogregations in the whole wor[illegible]. But the epiſtle is exhortatiue, for it inſtructeth the churches exhorteth and directeth.

And firſt in dede he ſheweth, who he is, from whome th[illegible] epiſtle procedeth, that he may geue authoritie therunto: an[illegible] maye declare alſo that he is the head of his churche, the B[illegible] ſhop, Duke and gouernour. That part is take of the imag[illegible] ſet forth in the firſte chapter. And followeth the prophetic[illegible] maner of ſpeaking: This ſayeth he whiche holdeth the ſeu[illegible] Starres in his righthade. For the Prophetes ſaye likewiſ[illegible] Thus ſayeth the lorde God of Iſraell, thus ſayeth the lor[illegible] of hoſtes. Thus ſayeth the Lorde, whiche brought thee out [illegible] Aegipte, &c. And two eſpeciall thinges he repeteth of the f[illegible] mer deſcription,[note 151] wherby he wil be knowen and do vs to v[illegible] derſtande, howe he beyng Lord and Biſhop ruleth and m[illegible] keth in his churche. Firſt he affirmeth that he holdeth in h[illegible] hand the ſeuen ſtarres. The hande is a token of working, [illegible] protection, or deliuerauce. The ſtarres we haue hearde to [illegible] the miniſters, and the miniſterie of the woorde, or the church Therfore Chriſt holdeth the miniſterie in the church, and [illegible] miniſters worketh the ſaluation of the faithfull. After he [illegible] firmeth that he walketh, not ſlepyng, or doyng nothing [illegible] the middes of ſeuen golden candelſtickes. In the middes [illegible] ſayed, to the ende we ſhould vnderſtand, that he geueth h[illegible] ſelfe indifferently to al men, and ruleth ouer al with like [illegible] and gouernement.[note 152] Fulwel wrote herof D. Fraunces lambe[illegible] What, ſaieth he is to walke or to be in the middes of cong[illegible] gations, but to aſſiſte them, kepe, inſtructe, helpe them, [illegible] by al meanes to watche ouer them. For the whiche cauſe [illegible] ſaieth alſo in the laſt of S. Matth. Beholde I am with you [illegible] waies vnto the worldes ende. Hereof you haue a moſte [illegible] figure in the lawe: wherin amonges other thinges whic[illegible] apperteined to the miniſterie of the highe prieſte, he had [illegible] charge of oyle and of ſeuen candels for thoſe muſt he pour[illegible] and ſnafte, and poure in oyle, when it wanted. So Chriſ[illegible] the highe and true Biſhop hath the charge of the ſeuen candelles, that is to ſaie, of al congregations: and is careful that they wante not that oyle, whiche is mentioned in the 44. Pſalm. He watcheth, that they wante not the fire and light of the veritie. Finally he ſnafteth and pourgeth by faithe, what thinge ſo euer hath nede to be pourged in them. Thus farre he. Whiche thinges when they heare, whiche make the Biſſhop of Rome head of the churche, It is maruell if by and by they vnderſtande not theyr folly and madnes. Here the lord addeth alſo, that he knoweth the workes to witte all bothe good and euil, aſwell of the Biſhop as of his Churche. For the Lord knoweth al thinges, and is head Biſhop of the Catholicke or vniuerſall Churche whiche alſo remembreth the thoughtes of al men in the world at one inſtaunte: who ſeeth what is donne, and what is not donne, and what thinges are nedefull, nothing eſcapeth him. And ſuche in dede ought he to be, that is head vniuerſall of his Church. And this ſentece is repeted, I knowe thy workes, in the beginninge of euery epiſtle. And verely it is full of comforte, when we heare that Chriſt knoweth all our doynges. For we beleue alſo that he hath a faithfull care of all our matters.

Now this great Biſhop comendeth ſome thinges in this congregation of Epheſus. For good workes in dede are allowed of Chriſt, and he praiſeth the ſame, to thende he might geue a ſpurre vnto ſuche as runne in his waye. For firſte he alloweth the labour and patience bothe of the Biſhop and Church.[note 153] Labour compriſeth thought and care in the waie of God, mortifiyng of the fleſh, ſtudy of good workes, but chiefly the croſſe and perſecution, whiche the ſtory teſtifieth to haue ben extreme and cruell in the time of Domitian. And excepte the perſecuted haue patience, they can not indure the labour. Holy patience kepeth vs in worke and holy labour.

But leeſte that patience ſhuld be ſtretched to thoſe things wherein to be impatient is accompted prayſe worthy,[note 154] he addeth the ſeconde poincte that he prayſeth in them, that thou canſt not beare euill men. And by theſe euill he meaneth not weaklings, or ſuch as erre without maliciouſnes: But the prophet Dauid ſaieth alſo, Pſalm. 119. I haue hated the wicked, thy law haue I loued. What we ſhuld do with the weake in the faith, or with them that erre of ignorauce, rather than of o[illegible] ſtinate ſtubbernes, the Apoſtle hath taughte vs in the 14. [illegible] the Romanes. The example of our Sauiour. Hath thaugh[illegible] alſo, bringing againe that ſtrayed Shepe vpon his ſhulde[illegible] into the ſhepefolde. Therefore the lord ſpeaketh here of t[illegible] obſtinate, of the diſceauers which delight to erre the ſelue[illegible] and to drawe others with them into errours, no Chriſte p[illegible] tience biddeth to beare with ſuche men.

And in the woordes followyng he declareth of what ſor[illegible] thoſe euill men were. And thou haſte examined them whi[illegible] ſaye they be Apoſtles, and be not, and haſte founde them[illegible] ayrs.[note 155] Lo he ſpeaketh of the falſe apoſtles, of whom in ſ. Ioh[illegible] time there was exceadyng great plentie. For they were [illegible] zareans mixing the lawe with grace, and attributing I[illegible] fication to the lawe and to our owne rightuouſnes. Wh[illegible] the holy and great counſell at Ieruſalem condemned, a[illegible] [illegible] pereth in the xv. chapter of the actes of the Apoſtles. S[illegible] a falſe Apoſtle was Hebion. Euſebius mentioneth in th[illegible] boke of the Eccleſiaſticall ſtory the xxvii. chapter. Here [illegible] was added Cerinthus that hereticke not Apoſtle. There [illegible] more alſo, whereof ſome denied the humanitie of Chri[illegible] ſome his deitie. Againſt whome wrote Iohn in his Goſ[illegible] and in his Epiſtle: And Ireney in the firſte boke againſt h[illegible] tickes. Theſe the Lorde denieth to be Apoſtles, or Apoſt[illegible] call: which the Apoſtles haue alſo denied Actes. 15. And [illegible] wiſe the Apoſtle S. Iohn in his epiſtle canonicall: who [illegible] liar, ſayeth he, but he that denieth Ieſus to be Chriſte? But if ſuche trouble were in Churches whileſt the Apoſ[illegible] were yet liuynge,[note 156] if there were than ſo manye diſceaue[illegible] what maruell is it, thoughe in the dregges of the worlde, witte in this our laſte time, there be not a fewe ſoun[illegible] where be they nowe that wraſte diſſentions and troubles the defence of theyr errour? The Goſpellers them ſel[illegible] ſaye they are at diſſention. God is God of concorde, he[illegible] than ſhoulde I beleue that God is amonge thoſe that [illegible] ſent? So might the Sophiſters alſo haue reaſoned in th[illegible] [illegible] poſtles time.

And here haue we a pefitte waye, in what ſorte the ch[illegible] ches ſhoulde worke, whileſte troubleſome perſons like f[illegible] Apoſtles attempte to diuide the Churche a ſonder.[note 157] For ſuch ringeleaders muſte be tried and examined: And tried muſte they be after the chriſten belefe, and doctrine of the Apoſtles, and inquiſition muſte be made, whether they be Apoſtles and trewe men, or falſe Apoſtles and lyars. When we ſhall haue founde them to be falſe Apoſtles and liars, and that they go forewarde obſtinately in theyr wickednes, they be not to be ſuffered: as in dede the Epheſians did not vouche ſafe to beare with ſuche trompers. And we muſte know that the Paſtors ought one waye, and the Chriſten Magiſtrate another waye, and the people the thirde waye not to abide open heretikes. For the Paſtour not only beareth not with them, in diſſemblyng and takinge hede to him ſelfe of thoſe wolues, but aſſayleth them with holeſome doctrine, and re [illegible]ulſeth them from the ſhepefoldes of Chriſte: But the Magiſtrate, bicauſe he is a chriſten Magiſtrate, and by his duty alſo, not only as a priuate perſon but alſo as a Magiſtrate, [illegible]ught to ſerue Chriſte, he aught alſo with the ſwoorde of [illegible]uſtice to driue awaye poyſon from the churche, and to pu [illegible]iſhe manifeſte blaſphemies.[note 158] And the people are commaun [illegible]ed, neyther to heare them, nor receiue them, nor to haue [illegible]ny thinge to do with heretickes, and ſo not to abide them. They maye therfore be aſhamed of their naughtineſſe, and [illegible]retence of their peruerſe patience, which thinke it no ſhame [illegible]o maynteyne Heretickes, and to flatter the manifeſte ene [illegible]ies of Chriſte and the Churche. Pſalm. 15. He is prayſed, [illegible]hiche maketh not muche of the wicked: that is to witte, [illegible]n whoſe ſighte the wicked manne is vile. Therefore is he [illegible]ghtly blamed, who ſo euer flattereth the vngodly. And [illegible] the hatred in dede is rather agaynſte wickednes, than a [illegible]aynſte the perſon of the wicked, whiche of it ſelfe is com [illegible]aunded to be loued. The Deuil at this daye reyſeth vp the [illegible]de hereſies of Hebion, Cerinthus, and of others in Terueto a [illegible]paniarde, and in the Anabaptiſtes, Libertines, and other [illegible]onſters, ſo that the thinge it ſelfe, and the daunger ther [illegible] commaundeth vs to watche, and to driue away the moſte [illegible]uell wolues from the holy Church of Chriſte, whiche ne [illegible]ertheleſſe do ſet forth nothing more than patience and cha [illegible]tie, for this intente verely that they might be ſpared, and might vnpuniſhed teache what they lifte againſt Chriſt, a[illegible] worke againſt his churche, yea teare it in pieces with th[illegible] wicked tethe.

But when theſe euil men are not ſuffered, but impugn[illegible] whiche ſeduce and are ſeduced,[note 159] a greate conflicte arriſe[illegible] wherof againe are labours, thoughtes, carefulnes, watc[illegible] ynges, iniuries to be ſuffered for the name of Chriſt, and d[illegible] fence of the veritie. For vnleſſe we be here diligent and p[illegible] tient, the diſceauers ouer come. But herin did the churc[illegible] of the Epheſians behaue them notably wel, in ſo muche th[illegible] the Lorde nowe commendeth exceadingly the magnani[illegible] tie, patience and conſtancie of the paſtour and of his churc[illegible] For neyther ought theſe thinges to be expounded, that [illegible] ſhoulde referre them vnto that patience, wherby euil me[illegible] are ſuffered, and permitted to procede in theyr malice a[illegible] diſceiptfulneſſe. For ſo ſhould this place ſtriue agaynſt th[illegible] thinges whiche went a little before. Whiche thinge the [illegible] mon interpretour minded as it ſemeth to haue eſchew[illegible] For thus he readeth, and thou haſte patience, and haſt ſuff[illegible] red, where it is in the Greke, and haſt ſuffered and haſt p[illegible] tience. He altered the order, and would not ſet, haſte ſuffer[illegible] before: but haſte patience: leſte any manne ſhoulde vnd[illegible] ſtande that they had ſuffered the falſe Apoſtles. But ſet b[illegible] fore patience and put after ſufferaunce: That we might v[illegible] derſtande that they ſuffered not euill menne, but euill wrought by euill men. So they with patience trauailed [illegible] Chriſtes name, to witte, to be mainteyned againſt naugh[illegible] hereſies. And he addeth, thou haſt not fainted, beyng wer[illegible] and broken with labours. For we are taught to ouerco[illegible] through patient conſtancie, whiche is rightly called in d[illegible] the accompliſhment of euery good worke.

All and euery of theſe thinges we muſte applie vnto [illegible] and vnderſtande with what thinges we maye nowe alſo this daye pleaſe Chriſt our redemer, king and Biſhoppe, truly we walke in the ſame ſteppes, wherin we ſee the co[illegible] gregation of the Epheſians to haue walked.

It followeth what thing he blamed in the ſame churc[illegible] that they haue lefte theyr firſte loue. What time they fir[illegible] receyued the Goſpell by S. Paul, and after by Iohn, a[illegible] other godly menne, there was ſene a great feruentnes in the wordes and dedes of the faithefull: whiche thinge maye be gathered bothe by the actes of the Apoſtles, and alſo by the epiſtle of S. Paul to the Epheſians. Thei loued God and their neighbours with a moſte feruent zeale. They burned in reforming of maners. But in proceſſe of time this heate was well couled, and they wared colder in trewe godlines. This greate miſchiefe he rebuketh in them, and as followeth deſireth to haue it redreſſed. And here let vs note howe not only reuolting Idolatrie, and other great crimes are imputed to the churche, but alſo if we ſlacke any thinge in holye zeale: ſo that herof we may learne, how holy and blameles we ought to be before God. Doubtles we can not here excuſe our ſelues before the diuine maieſtie, whiche were xxx. yeres paſte more ferueter in this cogregatio, than we be at this daye. &c. Our lord God lighten our mindes, that we may pleaſe him. To whome be glory.

## ¶ The ſeconde parte of the Epiſtle to the Epheſians where is ſpoken of Penaunce and of the Nicolaites. The Eight Sermon.

REmembre therefore from whence thou arte fallen, and repent, and do the firſte workes. Or els I will come vnto thee ſhortely, and will remoue thy Cadelſticke out of his [illegible]lace, except thou repet. But this thou haſt, [illegible]icauſe thou hateſt the dedes of the Nicolai [illegible]anes, which dedes I hate alſo. Let him that [illegible]ath eares, heare what the ſpirite ſayeth to [illegible]he congregations. To him that ouercometh wil I geue to eate of the tree of life, which is [illegible]n the middes of the Paradiſe of God.

[note 160]The accuſations of our Sauiour Chriſt, which he vſeth [illegible] gainſte his ſeruauntes that be ſinnes, tende not to this en[illegible] doubtles, that men ouerwhelmed with reproches might b[illegible] aſhamed, diſpaire, and periſh: but rather that thei ſhuld ame[illegible] be reſtored and liue. Therfore the Lorde Ieſus to his blameyn[illegible] by and by annexeth an exhortation to repentauce, that th[illegible] may be ſaued: And alſo deſcribeth the trew and lawful penau [illegible]

For we hearde what thing he rebuked in the cogregati[illegible] of the Epheſians: let vs heare nowe what the lord require[illegible] of the ſame, and how he ſeketh to haue the errour reforme[illegible] verely by repentaunce, to the whiche he exhorteth. For [illegible] haue ſayed that the Lord ſtriketh and healeth: chiefly in th[illegible] caſe. Whiche doctrine ſurely is proper and perpetuall to th[illegible] churche of Chriſt.

He mentioneth chiefely of three thinges in this matter, in his counſel or exhortation to amendement.[note 161] Firſte he co[illegible] ſelleth or exhorteth to remebre, [illegible] from whic[illegible] they are fallen: that is to ſaie, with how great loue they ha[illegible] burned hitherto, and nowe howe coulde they be wared: [illegible] how fortunate and bleſſed a ſtate they haue ſtande hither[illegible] and nowe in howe vnfortunate and ſhamefull they lie [illegible] For the acknoweledging of the treſpas is the beginning repentaunce,[note 162] if beyng illumined by faithe we conſider we[illegible] what benefites we haue loſte, and in what miſerie we no[illegible] are in. He that weneth he hathe loſte nothinge, will ne[illegible] be moued to make any ſearche or inquiſition, he that th[illegible] keth him ſelfe to haue fallen from no felicitie, he will thin[illegible] of nothyng how he maye be reſtored. Therefore in amen[illegible] ment of lyfe it behoueth that acknoweledgyng and conf[illegible] ſion of ſinnes go before, by the whyche we maye lament b[illegible] fore God our pouertie and miſerie. And in dede they fa[illegible] not from felicitie, whiche were neuer in any felicitie. The[illegible] fore ſaie we,[note 163] that holy menne maye fall, and alſo be reſtor[illegible] by repentaunce. Than after the acknoweledgyng of our [illegible] ſerie followeth repentaunce: that is the comynge agayne[illegible] our minde, that we goe not foorthe on alwayes like mad[illegible] menne and fooles, walkinge in the waye of vanitie an[illegible] vnrightuouſneſſe.[note 164] For repentaunce is a retourning a co[illegible] uerſion and chaunge, wherby we are tourned awaye fro[illegible] euill, and are conuerted to good, in retourning to our witte, and working rightuouſnes.

For laſt of all followeth that, whiche expoundeth this repentaunce. Do the fourmer workes: To witte be hote again in loue, worke the workes of faith, which are fruictes worthy of repentaunce. Beholde there nedeth no new lawes,[note 165] or long diſputations concerning amendement. Briefly, do the fourmer workes, not of the fleſhe, but thoſe whiche thou beganſt when thou firſt receiuedſt the Goſpel, and waſt borne again in Chriſt. Finally this is the true reformation, to do the fourmer workes of God. Not the latter, whiche the errour of the worlde hath deuiſed. This is verely, and ſhalbe the true repentaunce, to witte, the acknowledging of the ſinne, the conuerſion vnto God and to good, and a tourning away from the Deuill and from euill, and working of the firſt good, or Godly rightuouſnes of the faith in Chriſt. There be many and ſondry diſputations of repentaunce and partes of the ſame, of the contrition of the harte, confeſſion of the mouth, and ſatiſfaction of that worke: But lyke as there is none briefer tha this of Ieſus Chriſt, ſo verely is ther none better or more certain.

To theſe his exhortations and Godly coucelles he addeth threatenings moſt greuous, if haply perill might pearſe,[note 166] that loue could not ſtyre or moue. And he ſpeaketh but fewe wordes in dede. But he vnderſtandeth a great euill that can not be ſpoken nor declared. Except thou repent, I wyll remoue thy candelſtick out of his place. The candelſtick, as the lord him ſelfe hath expounded it, is the churche. Which ſtandeth in her place, whyleſt ſhe leaneth to Chriſt: and is of Chriſte preſerued, ſo long as the preaching of the veritie is in the ſame mainteined, and is in dede the churche of Chriſt: She is remoued out of her place, when ſhe is without the preachynge of the truthe, and now leaneth no more vpon Chriſt, nether is of hym defended, but is forſaken, and is no longer in dede the churche of Chriſt. This is done of Chriſt himſelfe, by his [illegible]uſt iudgement, what tyme our vnthanfulnes, and lyfe that can not repent, driueth God to depart from vs, to relinquiſh vs to our errour and darknes, and leaue vs to diſceauable men, &c. This ſenſe Aretas acknowledgeth, who: to remoue the church, ſaith he, is, when it is leſt bare and deſtitute of Gods grace: by reaſo of the which nakednes, it wauereth in doubtfull perplexitie and in ſtormes that are caſt to her by wicked men. And verely we ſe how at this day the churche of the Epheſians is remoued out of her place, and no longer inioyet[illegible] the holſome doctrine of Chriſt nor that it ſtandeth vpon th[illegible] holſome rocke Ieſus Chriſt[illegible] But is oppreſſed with the peſ[illegible] ferous doctrine or rather madnes of Mahomet, and lieth i[illegible] ſorrowe vnder the fete of the Turkes. We ſee at this day i[illegible] Germany (more is the pititie) many candelſtickes remoued out of their place, not without the great triumphe of Satha and loſſe of ſoules. Moreouer that ſame is alſo to be obſerued in this threatening, that without terrour he ſayth, I wil come to thee ſhortly. For it is a Phraſe of ſpeache. Fo[illegible] we ſay alſo,[note 167] I will come to thee by and by: That is, I wyl come to reuenge and puniſh, and that perauenture ſoner th[illegible] thou lokeſt for. Moſte certenly, when ſo euer I chaunce [illegible] come, I will take puniſhment of thee. Let no man therfo[illegible] thinke to eſcape vnpuniſhed in a life that can not repent.

[note 168]Again where the Lord repeteth, except thou repent, he plai[illegible] ly teſtifieth, that the boſome of Gods mercy and clemency, i[illegible] ready open, if we do penaunce, how ſoeuer we haue offended him before, in the meane time we learne here openly and moſ[illegible] certainly that we can by no counſelles or conſultations, [illegible] no armies nor policies preuaile one whit on our perils, [illegible] les we repet. Therfore vnles we wil haue our churches to [illegible] ſubuerted, and geuen ouer to be ſeduced and diſtroied of the deuill [illegible] his ſeducers, let vs repet in time, and receiue again the firſt loue.

[note 169]Again he comendeth the ſinguler vertu in this cogregat[illegible] eſpecially for that thei haue hated the doings of the Nicolait[illegible] which God him ſelfe alſo hateth. Let vs here marke euer[illegible] word. He ſaith not thou didſt ſlie, or eſchewe and contin[illegible] but thou haſt hated. The force of hatred is great, mouing [illegible] to perſecute, that thou hateſt.

[note 170]Moreouer he ſaith not, thou haſt hated the Nicolaita[illegible] but the workes of the Nicolaitans. For we ought to hate t[illegible] perſone of no man for it ſelf, but the vice in the man ſo the w[illegible] we ſhal forſake it, we ſhuld loue the ma with al our hart. A[illegible] that muſt nedes be a great euil, which God him ſelf cofeſſe[illegible] that he hateth. Here al congregations ſhal vnderſtand, that th[illegible] ought alſo to hate by al meanes the hereſie and abominatio of the Nicolaitans. Albeit that at this day the name be extingwiſhed yet the hereſie and abomination of the Nicolaitans remaineth.

This Nicolas was of Antioche, one of the ſeuen deacos, of whom mention is made in the.6. chap. of the Actes. He is ſaid to haue reuolted from the puritie of faith, as Iudas did: And where he was before a Gentile (for it is ſaid howe he was a Proſelyte) he retourned in certen thinges to gentilitie as a dog to his vomite. The Nicolaitas be alſo Gnoſtici, and conſortes of Carpocrates, filthie and moſt wicked people. Clemes excuſeth this Nicolas ſomwhat in Euſebius in the.3. boke. 29 chap. of the Eccleſiaſtical hiſtory. But that excuſe ſemeth not ſufficient or iuſt, ſince all the auncientes with one mouth accuſe the ſame, and namely the very iudgemet of God in this preſent and in the Epiſtles following. Ireneus condemneth him of this ſame place, in the firſt booke againſt the Valentinians chap. 27. &c. Tertulian in thend of Heret. preſcrip. toucheth here wittely the factes of the Nicolaitans, and deteſteth the ſame. Neuertheles he expoundeth them not, but paſſeth them ouer. And I know not how wittely Epiphanius hath vttered and declared in order the wicked and abhominable actes, neither to be thought nor told, and moſt beaſtly filthines, ſuch as hath not bene heard of the hereſie. 25.26.27. &. 31. &c. Philaſtrius alſo and S. Auſten haue touched the Nicolaitans, either of them in their regiſter of hereſies. Shamefaſt [illegible]es wil not ſuffer me to recite. It is enough, if we know that ſame which in the epiſtle to Pergamum the Lorde him ſelfe hath expounded, calling the doctrine of Nicolaitans, the doctrine of Balaa the ſouthſaier. But who knoweth not, what counſel he gaue to Balaac king of Moab and of Madian, and how he proſtituted faire wenches to the yong men of Iſrael, by whoſe acquaintaunce intiſed, they both defiled the ſelues with fornicatio, and did eat alſo of meates offered vp to Idols, being made partakers of Baalpeor. Let him that wil reade Ioſe [illegible]hus in the iiii. boke of Antiquities. cap. 6. And doubtles the [illegible]acrifices of the Nicolaitans ſeme to differ nothing from the [illegible]ecrettes of Priapus, or Gerecinthia, or the mother of God [illegible]es, and the nightly ſeruice of Bacchus. Ireneus ſignifieth [illegible]penly that the Carpocratites, who ar called alſo Gnoſtici, did not abhorre images, but paincted and facioned to the ſelue[illegible] the Images of Ieſus and Paule, with the Images of certi[illegible] Phyloſophers: And that the Image of Ieſu, as they talke, was made expreſſely of Pilate, who comaunded the face [illegible] Ieſus to be painted liuely. &c. But howſoeuer that was, th[illegible] is certain, that thactes of the Nicolaitans were euill report[illegible] of for their fornications and adulteries: And that the Nicol[illegible] tans abſteined not from images, nor from meates offred [illegible] idolles.[note 171] Againſt the which errour S. Paul wrote alſo ma[illegible] thynges.

Hereof let vs learne to abhorre and flie fornication, a[illegible] neuer to thinke of reſtoring the Stewes or other places [illegible] whoredom. Fie for ſhame. Let vs learne hereby to kepe h[illegible] virginitie, ſyngle lyfe, and lawfull mariages, flie thoſe d[illegible] the Nicolaitans, Let vs learne hereby, to kepe wel our ſel[illegible] from Idols, idolatry, and from all ſtraunge kyndes of w[illegible] ſhippinges. All thoſe God hateth.

[note 172]And with an acclamation he pearſeth the eares of al me[illegible] mouing al to attentiuenes and holy obedience. And appli[illegible] alſo this doctrine to all tymes and to all congregation[illegible] the world. He vſed his wonted ſpeache, repeted ſo ofte in t[illegible] Goſpell: He that hath eares to heare, let him heare. Not th[illegible] it is in our ſtrength to heare and obey God. For God pr[illegible] pareth our eares, and with his grace frameth and draw[illegible] our hartes. And let them to whom the grace of God is g[illegible] ted, beware leaſt through their negligence, vanitie, and lig[illegible] nes they loſe it not. Let them ſhewe ſuch dilligence, as G[illegible] in his worde requireth and preſcribeth. They that do th[illegible] haue eares to heare. He ſayth therfore, take the hede to wh[illegible] God now ſpeaketh, and whoſe hartes nowe he ſtyreth a[illegible] moueth, that you loſe not this grace through your neglig[illegible] be diligent, attentife, and circumſpect, ſtyryng vp in yo[illegible] ſelues the gift of God.

[note 173]Now alſo he prouoketh to dilligece by authoritie diui[illegible] The ſpirite of God ſpeaketh and reuealeth theſe things, [illegible] the ſpirite of men or of errour, for God ſpeaketh by his ſ[illegible] rite, whiche is red to be the ſpirite both of the father and o[illegible] ſonne. Moreouer he applieth all and euery thing to all co[illegible] gregations, where he ſayth, what the ſpirite ſaith to the co[illegible] gregations, not to the congregation. It is now than manifeſt, and out of all controuerſie,[note 174] that thoſe ſeuen churches do repreſent a figure of al churches throughout the whole world, and that all they be inſtructed in thoſe ſeuen.

Furthermore leaſt any thyng ſhoulde wante to the iuſte exhortation vnto repentaunce, to faith and dilligence, laſt he annexeth a moſte ample promyſe, and vſeth an allegoricall ſpeache, that it might haue the more grace with it.[note 175] To them that ouercome he promyſeth to geue the fruict of the tree of lyfe, planted in the paradiſe of God. And alludeth to the.2. Chap. of Geneſis. And he tranſlateth the ſenſe from earthly thinges to celeſtiall. The paradiſe[note 176] of God (by the which ſom vnderſtande the church) is that eurlaſting bleſſe and felicitie, wherof the Lorde ſpake to the thief, ſaying: This day ſhalt thou be with me in paradiſe. Herein is the tree of lyfe Chriſt, communicating to vs his eternal life: Whiche we inioy and haue the fruition of, whileſt being conueied into heauen by hym and with hym we liue. Finally this is that Ambroſia or Godly drinke, which the heauenly father geueth vs to drinke. But this great and wonderfull good chaunceth not to euery one, but only to him that ouercometh. For Adam had not ouercome, but vanquiſhed had died. If we therfore ſhall ouercome the fleſh, the Deuil and the world, and that through Chriſt, we ſhal liue alſo in the world to come with Chriſt.

The complutention boke hath, whiche is in the middes of [illegible]he Paradiſe of my God. And Aretas expoundeth it,[note 177] and [illegible]ayth: Let no man herewith be offended. Al humble thinges [illegible]gree to the diſpenſation of the incarnation, whiche was made for our cauſe, ſince that he himſelf in the Goſpel ſaith: [illegible] aſcende vnto my father, and your father, to my God, and [illegible]o your God. &c.

And thus farre hetherto concerning the Epiſtle of Ieſus Chriſt by Iohn to the Epheſians, and what profit our churches alſo, and euery of vs may receiue therof. The Lorde lyghten the eyes of our mynde.

## ¶ The ſecond Epiſtle of Ieſu Chriſt by Iohn to them of Smyrna is expounded. And is an exhortati [illegible][illegible] to patience, and conſolation in afflictions. The ix. Sermon.

ANd vnto the Aungel of the cogr[illegible] gation of Smyrna wryte. Theſ[illegible] thinges ſayth he that is firſt an[illegible] the laſt, whiche was dead and i[illegible] aliue. I know thy workes, and t[illegible] bulations and pouertie, but thou arte rich[illegible] And I know the blaſphemie of them which call them ſelues Iewes, and are not: but a[illegible] the congregation of Sathan. Feare none [illegible] thoſe things which thou ſhalt ſuffer. Behol[illegible] the Deuil ſhall caſt ſome of you into priſo[illegible] to tempte you, and you ſhall haue tribulat[illegible] ten daies. Be faithfull vnto the death, and [illegible] will geue thee a crowne of lyfe. Let him th[illegible] hath eares, heare, what the ſpirite ſaith to congregations, he that ouercometh ſhall n[illegible] be hurt of the ſecond death.

[note 178]Ieſus Chriſt from the right hand of the father, throu[illegible] the miniſterie of an aungell by the Apoſtle and Euange[illegible] S. Iohn, exhorteth the congregations of Smyrna, than [illegible] flicted with all kinde of euils for the worde of God, vnto [illegible] feraunce, and comforteth the ſame ſighing nowe vnder [illegible] croſſe, promiſing great thinges to them that ouercome. A[illegible] verely ther can not of this maner and in this matter a be[illegible] or briefer exhortation and conſolation be found. For in [illegible] wyſe it is couched of the eternal wiſdome of the father, [illegible] vnto all times,[note 179] and to all that mourne vnder the croſſe it [illegible] right well agree. For like as Chriſt at the right hande of [illegible] father is the catholique or vniuerſal Byſhop: ſo verely is [illegible] doctrine generall, which he him ſelfe alſo applieth to all c[illegible] gregations in the ende of this Epiſtle, and in others. And ſ[illegible] he declareth that he loueth his churche, and is preſent in the ſame by his power and ayde.

And verely it is to be marueled,[note 180] that nothing is blamed in this churche, ſince that ſome faulte is founde in maner with all others. Therfore was the churche of Smyrna right excellent, howbeit not without any ſpirite. For the Lorde of his goodnes doth not impute vnto vs ſmal faultes (of the which the Prophet ſpeaketh, who ſhall ſay, my heart is cleane? And from my hidde ſinnes clenſe me) ſo that there be a feruent deſyre or zeale of Godlines in vs, and that we be voyde of great enormities.

Firſt is ſhewed, vnto whom this heauenly letter is ſent,[note 181] to the Paſtour of the churche of Smyrna, and to the whole flocke. For the captayne is ſayd to haue ſoughten or fled, or to haue taken peace, when the whole armie together with him hath done this. And the ſtories beare witnes that Policarpus was that ſame meſſenger or paſtour of the church of Smyrna, ordeined of the Apoſtles the ſelues,[note 182] namely of S. Iohn, Byſhop there, and that he liued in the miſterie of this congregation lxxxvi. yeares. For ſo many he accompteth hym ſelfe before the Lieftenaunt Herode, what tyme he was brought to execution. For in the fourth perſecution of the churche, Aurelius Antoninus, and Aurelius Comodus, being Emperoures, he was taken and brought to the gouernour: And at length for the open and ſincere confeſſinge of Chriſt he was burnt. He had this very muche in his mouth: That nothing ought to be receiued for true, vnleſſe it were knowen to be ſet forth by the Apoſtles. Ireneus affirmeth that when he was a childe he ſawe this olde father a man of great yeares and reuerece, in the third boke and third chapt. againſt hereſies, where he telleth many thinges of him beſides. As alſo doth Euſebius in the iiii. boke of theccle. hiſtory [illegible]he xiiii. and xv. chapters. And S. Hierom in the regiſter of [illegible]he famouſe wryters of the Churche. Euſebius in his Chro [illegible]icis, noteth that he ſuffered Martyrdome in the yeare of [illegible]ur Lorde a. C ixx. Whereby it appeareth that he was [illegible]ade Byſhop of Smyrna in the yeare of our Lorde lxxxiiii. [illegible]r there about. For we ſayd euen nowe, that he had bene in that miniſterie lxxxvi. yeares. And therfore had he bene Byſhop of Smyrna many yeares before the ſetting forth of th[illegible] Apocalipſe whiche was written in the xcvii. yeare, would[illegible] God all Paſtours would ſet before their eies this good Policarpus to be followed, of whome there remayneth a not[illegible] ble Epiſtle to the Philippians.

After againe is the authour of the Epiſtle[note 183] declared, whic[illegible] is ſet forth with two titles, taken out of the fourmer viſi[illegible] of Iohn and deſcriptio of Chriſt. Thus ſaith the firſt and [illegible] laſt. &c. Wherby is ſignified the eternall diuinitie of Chriſt[illegible] which wanteth beginning and ending. And of him ſelfe is [illegible] uerlaſting. There is added, that he was dead, and liueth again[illegible] that is to witte, hath ryſen from the dead. And this begi[illegible] ning accordeth right well to the matter. For they perceiu[illegible] that whoſoeuer are afflicted for Chriſt and his Goſpell of m[illegible] mightie kinges and princes, haue a Lorde and patrone m[illegible] mightie and more faithfull, whiche in no wyſe can be ou[illegible] comen. Who can alſo in death kepe his, lyke as he reyſed [illegible] Chriſt from the dead, to the intent we myght haue an op[illegible] teſtimony, that we ſhal liue with Chriſt, euen in death it ſe[illegible]

[note 184]And now cometh he to the matter it ſelfe, and the whic[illegible] thing he repeteth in al Epiſtles, he ſayth here alſo: I kno[illegible] thy workes, to witte both good and euill. Thinke not that [illegible] neither know nor care for thy matters. Thou art verely ro[illegible] ten out in my handes, I know, ſee and care for thee and [illegible] thine. And theſe thinges boeth prouoke vs maruelouſly vertue, when we knowe that we haue God a loker on, [illegible] hath a care of vs: And alſo comforte vs greatly, which vnd[illegible] ſtande, how he that loueth vs, and in no matter neglecte[illegible] hath vs alwayes as it were before his eyes.

And here particularly he declareth what he knewe: A[illegible] firſt in dede the afflictions,[note 185] which verely they ſuffered in t[illegible] preſent perſecution of the Emperour Domitian. And aff[illegible] tion is as it were a generall worde, to the foure kindes fo[illegible] wyng. For he rehearſeth, touching their ſubſtaunce the ſ[illegible] ling of their goodes, and their pouertie: In their name [illegible] eſtimation, cotumelies, reproches, or blaſphemies: In bo[illegible] impriſonment, and bondes, yea and death alſo. For [illegible] theſe afflictions Godly men are exerciſed, for the truth ſa[illegible] of the wicked. And in theſe may be copryſed al other kindes of tribulation. The whiche the Epiſtle of Ieſus Chriſt reciteth in a Godly order. There is nothinge therfore of theſe matters whiche the Lord Chriſt knoweth not.

Pouertie hath the firſt place.[note 186] Neither ought we to take it here ſpiritually for the modeſtie and humblenes of mynde: although it be certaine, that the churche of Smyrna wanted not the ſame vertue: But there was pouertie and lacke of all things by reaſon of the ſpoiling of their goodes. For in time of perſecution, by vertue of kings proclamatios, the goodes of the faithful profeſſours of Chriſt ar cofiſcated to the kings vſe, or permitted to the ſoldiours, nobles, or promoters to take at their pleaſure: The faithful thruſt out of their houſes, ar either driuen into exile, or go a begging: would God we wanted examples therof at this day. Let vs herof learne to beare and ſuffer paciently the like chaunces alſo, being perſwaded that God knoweth our neceſſitie. And becauſe it is an hard thing [illegible]or an honeſt man to honger and want with his familie, for a [illegible]omfort and conſolation he addeth, but thou art riche.

This to the world ſemeth a paradoxe, or incredible.[note 187] What wyll they ſay, is he ryche that hath nothyng, and is brought [illegible]o the ſtate of beggars? There be doubtles goodes and ry [illegible]hes of the mynde muche better than corporall ſubſtaunce.

For this may be had, without the true felicitie, of ryche men [illegible]f this worlde, that liue a moſte miſerable lyfe. Agayne you [illegible]hall ſee a poore man, concerning worldly goodes, but fur [illegible]iſhed with the rycheſſe of the mynde, for this cauſe only to [illegible]e happy and moſte bleſſed. He coueteth nothyng, he is con [illegible]ent with his vocation: Neither would he chaunge his ſtate with moſte welthie and ryche kynges. Contrariwyſe you [illegible]hall ſee ryche men but of an euyll conſcience, and therfore [illegible]houghtfull and burthened with cares, and neuer mery. You [illegible]hall ſee poore men, but with mery hartes to leade a ioyfull [illegible]fe. Why than ſhoulde it ſeme maruell, yf he that is ſpoy [illegible]ed of his worldly goodes for Chriſte, and inriched with the [illegible]iftes of the minde, is glad and reioyſeth in God, and taketh a good parte al chaunces, and for the ſame cauſe is iudged [illegible]o be verely ryche? Doubtles the wyſe men of this worlde [illegible]we alſo, that the only wyſe man is trewly ryche. Which is gallauntly diſcourſed of Cicero. Aretas ſayth, in ſpiritual matters hauing a treaſure hidde in the fielde of thy harte, which is Chriſt, by reaſon of who thou art ryche alſo: Sinn[illegible] thou haſt him thy protectour, who alſo when he was ryche, for vs became poore. &c.

[note 188]In the ſecond place is recited blaſphemie, by the which[illegible] we vnderſtande all maner of raylinges and ſclauders, whe[illegible] by the name and eſtimation of the faithfull is hurte. Of th[illegible] whiche ſorte are theſe: They be heretickes and Schiſmatic[illegible] ſo many as be fauourers of this religion: They be wicke[illegible] people, diſpiſers of God and his ſaintes, the ennemies of [illegible] Gods ſeruice, and therfore the plages of the commo wealt[illegible] which if they be ſuffered, the common wealth muſt nedes [illegible] diſtroyed. And theſe thinges in dede many times vexe go[illegible] men more greuouſly, than the loſſe of their goodes. For w[illegible] wyll not ſet more by a good name, than by great ryche[illegible] Therfore the Lorde in Goſpell of S. Math. the.10. Chapt[illegible] With many wordes healeth this diſeaſe: And exhorteth [illegible] that for the auoyding of that infamie, they ſhould comit[illegible] thing vnworthy the name of chriſtians.

[note 189]In the meane tyme he declareth alſo, what moued the [illegible] thours of this miſchief, whome he blameth alſo exceadyn[illegible] to thintent that the godly ſhuld vnderſtad, how greatly th[illegible] ennemies of all Godlines are of God miſliked: And the[illegible] might alſo care the leſſe for their hatred and perſecutio. T[illegible] ſaye in dede, that they be Iewes, where they be nothing [illegible] Thus alſo S. Paul handled the Iewes in the ii. to the [illegible] mains. The Iewes are called confeſſours, honorers, and faithful ſeruauts of God. But theſe blaſpheme Gods na[illegible] they impugne the true faith, and oppreſſe them that prof[illegible] and worſhyppe God. Therfore be they not Iewes. W[illegible] than? The Synagoge, congregation or aſſemblee of Sa[illegible] Thus the very ſonne of God plucketh of the viſerne fr[illegible] theſe varlettes, to the comforte of all thoſe that ſuffer pe[illegible] cution, of them that ſet forth them ſelues with ſtoute titles the ende it ſhuld neuer greue them, that they are condem[illegible] of ſuche harlottes, the children of the Deuill. Chriſt att[illegible] teth to them the true title, and calleth them not the o[illegible] holy, and catholicke Churche of God, but the conſpir[illegible] and ſchole of Sathan, as in whome, not the ſpirite of God, but of Sathan, inſpireth lyes, iuggelinges, diſceiptes, blaſphemies, fyres and deathes. Therfore let it not greue thee at this daye, [illegible]n caſe it be thy fortune to be condemned for the Goſpell, of thoſe that call them ſelues moſte holy, moſte ſhining, moſte reuerent, and moſt irreprehenſible Prelates and Patrones of the olde churche, religion, and catholique faith, whiche haue on their ſyde, counſelles, fathers, ſo many ſucceſſions of Biſhoppes, the preſcript of ſo long time, and conſent of ſo many Realmes. They be nothynge leſſe than that they deſyre to be called: But rather the champions of Antichriſte, and the profeſſed ennemies, and treaders vnder feete of all chriſten pietie. For whome is prepared euerlaſting deſtruction.

After this he putteth an exhortation and a conſolation moſte euident, before the whiche ſetting alſo the ſonne,[note 190] he ſayth, feare nothyng of all that thou ſhalt ſuffer. The ſonne of God hym ſelfe feared the croſſe and death, and it is a naturall thyng to feare euilles, and death. Therfore we are not commaunded, that we ſhoulde non be men, and that lyke [illegible]toickes we ſhoulde ſaye howe the ſame thynges greue vs not, whiche neuertheleſſe tourment vs exceadingly: but [illegible]he faithfull are incouraged, that they ſhould ſtande ſtrong [illegible]n the fayth, neyther that they ſhould doe any thyng vnwor [illegible]hy the ſame for feare of puniſhement. We be therfore com [illegible]aunded boldly and cherely to contemne or ſuppreſſe feare, [illegible]nd to craue ſtrength by the ſpirite of God, and to exerciſe it [illegible] temptations.

There followe reaſons,[note 191] wherby he may obteine that he [illegible]ath perſwaded, may confirme, comforte and exhorte them [illegible] pacience and conſtancie. He prophecieth therfore to the Godly, what thing they ſhall ſuffer: And toucheth alſo the [illegible]hird kynde of affliction, impriſonment and bondes, vnder [illegible]hiche we vnderſtande all puniſhementes wherby our bo [illegible]ies are tourmented. But to be warned before of the euill, is great benefite. We are more eaſely ouercome of vnproui [illegible]ed perilles. And therfore the Lorde in the Goſpell after S. [illegible]ath. the x. Chapt. and after Iohn in the xv. and xvi. Chapters [illegible]lleth his diſciples of many euilles that ſhould come vnto them, and addeth therto: Theſe thinges haue I ſpoken to you, that when the tyme ſhall come, the myght remembr[illegible] them, that I haue tolde you before. So nowe alſo faithfully warneth the faithfull in this Epiſtle.

[note 192]And he toucheth the authour of theſe euilles, ſaying: Th[illegible] Deuill wyll caſt ſome of you into pryſon. Therfore we perceiue that thoſe euillies ariſe of the commo ennemy of mankynde, and of the ſaluation of the faythfull: Wherof we may coniecture, that he goeth about to intercept our ſaluation, and that we ought therfore to ſtande more earſtly agaynſt hym. The ſouldiours when they heare that their olde ennemy is at hande, waxe not ſluggyſhe, but cherefull. But the Deuyll inſpireth euill men, corrupteth Princes and Mag[illegible] ſtrates, whiche attempte perſecution againſt the churche. S[illegible] we reade that Sathan afflicted Iob, that is to haue prou[illegible] ked the Chaldeis and Sabeis to kill his ſeruautes and dri[illegible] away his cattell. Here therfore they may ſee, with what ſ[illegible] rite they are incouraged, whiche at this daye perſecute t[illegible] churche of Chriſt, for the profeſſion of the veritie. The Go[illegible] ly haue that whiche may comfort them: For they heare th[illegible] the ſame filthie beaſt is ſet againſt them, whiche ſo oft be[illegible] vanquyſhed of Chriſt the Prince of the faithfull, and of t[illegible] faithfull through Chriſtes ayde, may without any difficul[illegible] be ouercome. And verely the Lorde permitteth to the De[illegible] and deuelyſhemen power ouer his ſeruauntes. If thou m[illegible] uell why,[note 193] heare: That you may be tempted. God permitte[illegible] not his to Sathan, that they ſhould peryſhe, but that t[illegible] ſhould be tempted and tried. Therfore to a good ende are [illegible] deliuered to the fyre, that we might be pourged from our thines, that the vertu of our faith mought ſhine, and G[illegible] might be glorified, and we made the purer. Who therf[illegible] wyll hereafter be impatient, when we heare that we for t[illegible] great good are put to euill? We reade in the iii. of Wiſdo[illegible] As golde is tried in the fire, ſo are the faythfull proued. T[illegible] parable hath S. Peter expounded at large in the iiii. Cha[illegible] of the firſt Epiſtle.[note 194] Where he that wyll may haue it mor[illegible] boundauntly.

Moreouer the tyme alſo of tribulation is aſſigned [illegible] that for ten dayes. The tenth nombre ſignifieth a multitu[illegible] For Iacob ſayeth to his father in Lawe: Ten tymes haſte thou chaunged my wages. Geneſis xxxi. and Numeri xiiii. He ſayth he was tempted ten tymes, that is often, and many tymes. Iob alſo affirmeth hym ſelfe in the xxix. chapter. To haue bene iniuried ten tymes. The Lorde therfore ſayth at this preſent. You ſhalbe diuerſly and muche moleſted with euilles. Notwithſtanding foraſmuche as he putteth not monethes, yeares, nor ages, but dayes, he prophecieth that the euilles ſhall not be continuall, but that there ſhall always be ſpaces be twirte to breathe in, verely for the ſhortnes of perſecution, firſte Eſay the.26. Chapter. Secondly S. Peter in the firſt. Petri. 1. Do comforte the faithfull. It is the parte of the faithfull not to preſcribe God: But whether we be put to payne a long tyme or ſhorte, to take it patietly. Let vs thinke rather, that in the long continuaunce of euilles, there is ſome ende alſo forſene of the Lorde: And that in the ſame tyme of breathing, we muſte repare the euylles, and retourne vnto battell.

Laſte,[note 195] are the Godly incouraged by a moſte ample and large promyſe, in the whiche is alſo included the fourth and moſte greuouſe kind of affliction, alſo the bitter death it ſelfe, through fyre, halter, ſword, water. &c. But in caſe thou be not affraide of death, but vanquiſhing it alſo, ſhall offer vp thy ſelfe vnto God, than wil I geue thee, ſaith the Lord, a crown of life. Hereunto is annexed the ſtate of the Epiſtle, and ſome of all. Therfore be thou faithfull, cherefull, conſtant, euen to the very death, For the Lorde ſaith alſo in the Goſpel: Who ſo perſeuereth to the end, he ſhalbe ſaued.
